heading: How Do I Get on Birth Control
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Want to take charge of your family planning? Read our 101 on birth control to discover your choices and make the right decision for you!
tags: birth control; contraception; IUD vs birth control pills; oral contraceptives; vaginal ring
full_text:
To access birth control, you’ll need to talk to your healthcare provider, doctor, or clinic for a prescription. Or – for condoms, certain pills, emergency contraception, etc. – visit a drugstore or use an online retailer.

Birth control – or contraception – is critical for women’s reproductive health because it empowers us to plan our families and manage our well-being. In this guide, we’ll demystify the complex world of birth control to help you understand and navigate your options so you can make informed decisions that align with your personal and health goals.

As this is a 101 type of post, let’s start by understanding the diverse range of birth control methods available. You’re sure to be able to find one that best suits your individual needs if you know about them all. So – here goes!

Birth control pills to swallow are a popular choice and come in two types:

The pill is highly effective with perfect use, but it has to be taken at the same time every day to maintain its level of efficacy. This means a little bit of discipline on your part! So – the best time of day to take them is when you can most easily remember. This might be with your morning coffee or right before your nightly routine. For example, we’ve found some of our patients rubber band the pill pack around their toothpaste to prompt them to take it every morning! Whatever works for you is good.

However, in addition to birth control, the pill can provide benefits such as reduced risk of certain cancers, less severe menstrual cramps, and improvement in acne.

IUDs offer long-lasting, reversible contraception. The hormonal IUDs can also help with heavy menstrual bleeding and provide some protection against pelvic infections. The copper IUD is hormone-free and ideal for women who prefer a method without the side effects of hormones.

The patch is a simple, once-a-week application that provides the same hormonal protection as pills. To be most effective, you have to apply it on the same day each week and ensure you press it firmly against your skin with no gaps. If the patch becomes loose or falls off, you should replace it immediately to maintain protection against pregnancy.

The patch is an excellent option for you if you might have trouble remembering to take a pill every day but can definitely manage a weekly system. It can also help regulate menstrual cycles and decrease menstrual cramps. It’s effective immediately if started during the first day of your menstrual cycle.

The vaginal ring is a flexible, plastic ring inserted into your vagina for three weeks at a time, releasing estrogen and progestin to prevent ovulation. You remove the ring for one week to allow for a menstrual period before inserting a new ring. This offers a good balance of hormones and allows for their direct absorption, with fewer side effects compared to oral contraceptives.

Contraceptive injections are given every three months. This removes the possibility of user error associated with daily or weekly contraceptive methods. However, it can cause irregular menstrual cycles initially, and it may take some time for fertility to return after you stop the injections. Bear this in mind if you plan to start a family soon.

Implants are small, thin rods about the size of a matchstick inserted under the skin of your upper arm. They release a steady dose of progestin to prevent ovulation. Implants are effective for up to three to five years, making them one of the most preferred long-term methods.

Condoms are the only method offering dual protection against both pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections (STIs). They are a barrier contraception made from latex or polyurethane that covers the penis during sexual intercourse. Condoms are easy to obtain and use, making them a staple in sexual health strategies.

This shallow, dome-shaped cup is made of silicone and is inserted into the vagina to cover the cervix, preventing sperm from entering the uterus. Used with spermicide, diaphragms are a non-hormonal method of contraception that can be inserted several hours before intercourse. They require fitting by a healthcare provider to ensure the correct size and are a reusable, cost-effective choice if you prefer a hormone-free method.

Oral contraceptives and IUDs are some of the most popular choices for birth control for women, each with its pros and cons.

While both offer high levels of protection against pregnancy, when examining the pill vs. IUD effectiveness in real-life scenarios, we see a notable difference. The IUD boasts a more than 99% effectiveness rate because it eliminates user error – a significant factor with pills, where typical use (i.e. forgetting a pill occasionally!) drops effectiveness to around 91%. Otherwise, the effectiveness is also around 99%.

If you’re deciding between an IUD or birth control pills, the final choice often comes down to personal circumstances and preferences:

Hormonal fluctuations from birth control can result in a variety of side effects, which vary among users:

This is something you can discuss with an experienced women’s healthcare provider. They’ll help you choose the best option for you.

If you’re wondering how to combat fatigue from birth control, maintaining a healthy lifestyle is key. Regular physical activity and a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains can boost energy levels and overall health. If symptoms persist, you should discuss an alternative form of birth control with your healthcare provider.

Upon stopping birth control, you might notice a return to your natural menstrual cycle, which could be more irregular and possibly heavier than when on birth control.

You may also experience a temporary delay in fertility as your body adjusts.

Additionally, gaining weight after stopping birth control is a concern for some women, although this doesn’t affect everyone.

Navigating birth control options can seem daunting, but with the right information, you can confidently choose a method that suits your health and lifestyle needs. At Stella Mattina, we excel at offering exceptional care to women – both primary care and Ob/Gyn care.

We also pride ourselves on being easy to access and accept most insurances. Schedule an appointment and let’s talk about your birth control needs.

heading: How Do We Prevent Heart Disease?
author: Asma Bhaidani, PA-C
meta_description: Heart disease is one of the leading causes of illness for adults in the U.S. Learn how to prevent heart disease: basics, signs, strategies.
tags: everyday ways to prevent heart disease; heart disease vs. congenital heart disease; signs of heart disease
full_text:
Heart disease remains one of the leading causes of illness for adults in the U.S. The question of how we prevent heart disease matters if we want to live healthier lives, not just longer ones.

The good news is that many risk factors are modifiable.

Preventive care, everyday habits, and early awareness all play a role in helping you reduce your risk over time.

Understanding what heart disease is, how it develops, and what warning signs to watch for can make prevention feel more practical and achievable for you.

Heart disease is a broad term used to describe conditions that affect how your heart functions. Many heart-related problems develop gradually, influenced by your lifestyle, your genetics, and any underlying health conditions you have.

Preventive care from your Primary Care doctor focuses on identifying risk early and supporting your heart health before serious problems develop.

Heart disease and congenital heart disease are not the same.

Congenital heart disease refers to structural heart conditions present at birth.

Heart disease, on the other hand, typically develops later in life and is often linked to factors such as high blood pressure, high cholesterol, diabetes, smoking, and inactivity.

This distinction matters to you in the context of preventing heart disease because prevention strategies mainly apply to making sure you don’t acquire heart disease, not congenital conditions.

High blood pressure is not classified as heart disease itself, but it is one of the strongest risk factors for developing it.

Over time, elevated blood pressure places strain on your heart and blood vessels, which increases the likelihood of heart-related complications developing.

But that’s something you can help prevent if you manage your blood pressure through lifestyle changes and regular checkups.

This is a key point. As a society, we’ve become used to sitting down a lot! It’s comfortable and our work often demands it.

But regular physical activity will support your heart health in multiple ways. For example, it:

Movement also contributes to maintaining a healthy weight for you and managing stress – both important factors for good cardiovascular health.

Physical activity doesn’t have to mean intense workouts.

Consistent walking, cycling, swimming, or other moderate activities can make a meaningful difference when done regularly. You’re aiming for sustainability, not perfection.

Recognizing early warning signs can lead to earlier evaluation and care so you stay healthy.

Common signs of heart disease may include chest discomfort, shortness of breath, palpitations, or unexplained fatigue. Symptoms can appear gradually and may not always feel dramatic, which is why people sometimes overlook them.

Both men and women can experience classic heart-related symptoms, such as chest pressure or pain. However, women are more likely to also experience less typical symptoms, including nausea, unusual fatigue, dizziness, or pain in the jaw, neck, or back.

Men may be more likely to report chest pain as a primary symptom, while women may notice subtler changes.

These differences don’t mean heart disease looks entirely different in women and men, but they do highlight why paying attention to any persistent or unusual symptoms matters – regardless of gender.

Daily habits play a significant role in helping prevent heart disease. If you can make small, consistent changes, you’ll probably have more chance of staying healthy than trying for drastic changes that you then forget to keep up with.

So what preventive strategies do we advise at Stella Mattina?

1 Try to stay physically active on and off all day. If you work at a desk, get up and move every hour.

2 Eat a balanced diet choosing whole foods over ultra-processed ones where possible. These help your gut stay healthy, which can support weight loss or weight management.

3 Manage your stress levels. Meditation, coloring, calm breathing – anything that helps reset your system to normal.

4 Avoid smoking and, as much as possible, avoid inhaling smoke. These are major causes of heart problems.

5 Keep up with routine medical visits. Preventive screenings, such as blood pressure and cholesterol checks, help identify risk early.

February is Heart Month, a time dedicated to raising awareness about heart health and prevention. It serves as a reminder for us all to schedule checkups, start or revisit healthy habits, and have conversations about family history and risk factors.

The aim of Heart Awareness Month is to emphasize the value of prevention, education, and early action rather than wait for symptoms to appear.

If you have a family history of heart disease, high blood pressure, diabetes, or concerning symptoms, it’s worth discussing heart health with your doctor.

Our experience at Stella Mattina is that preventive care is most effective when it’s personalized, proactive, and ongoing. And that’s what we’re passionate about: keeping you healthy.

With several locations to choose from and same-day appointments available, we make it as easy as possible to work around your busy schedule and keep your heart healthy all year. Schedule your appointment with one of our primary care clinics here.

heading: How to Balance Hormones After Pregnancy
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Wanting to balance hormones after pregnancy to stabilize your mood? Learn how diet and perhaps natural hormone therapy can help you.
tags: balance hormones after pregnancy; BioTe hormone therapy; signs of hormone imbalance after pregnancy
full_text:
Pregnancy is an extraordinary journey, but as your body adjusts to the postpartum phase, you might find yourself experiencing hormonal fluctuations. In this article, we’ll look at the changes you might notice and strategies to help you balance hormones after pregnancy – embracing natural and healthy approaches.

Your body goes through significant hormone changes after pregnancy that can impact your physical and emotional well-being. Understanding them is important for your recovery. Let’s look at them in turn and see what symptoms they might cause. Knowledge is power!

Estrogen plays a part in various bodily functions, including bone health and skin elasticity, while progesterone supports pregnancy and then decreases postpartum. Both these hormones experience a significant drop after birth. Here are the signs to look out for:

Mood swings: The decrease in estrogen and progesterone can contribute to a rollercoaster of emotions for you, making you feel ecstatic one moment and tearful the next. You’re not losing the plot! It’s natural after pregnancy.

Fatigue: Alongside the demands of caring for a newborn, the hormone drop can make you feel more tired than usual.

Hair loss: High estrogen levels during pregnancy reduce hair shedding and keep your hair lush. A postpartum drop in estrogen can cause noticeable hair loss, which can be distressing – but the good news is it’s typically temporary.

Irregular menstrual cycles: It’s normal for your cycle to be unpredictable as it finds its new rhythm, especially if you’re breastfeeding. Try not to worry about it, because stress affects your hormones too!

Postpartum thyroiditis may occur and lead you to have fluctuating thyroid hormone levels that contribute to tiredness and emotional changes, mirroring those symptoms of estrogen and progesterone imbalance.

Nursing your baby is not just a beautiful way to bond but also plays a role in your hormonal balance postpartum. Breastfeeding triggers the release of several hormones, and understanding their role will point to strategies for you to help balance hormones after pregnancy.

Prolactin levels increase throughout pregnancy and each time you nurse, maintaining your milk supply. It also has a calming effect, enhances maternal behaviors, and strengthens the emotional bonding with your baby.

If you’re breastfeeding, prolactin remains elevated and can influence other hormones. You may have a delayed return of regular menstrual cycles, and some women even experience amenorrhea (absence of periods) while nursing. However, this effect, while serving as a natural contraception method, is not foolproof! Ovulation may resume unpredictably. You should still take precautions to avoid a further pregnancy until you’re ready.

Oxytocin, known as the “love hormone,” facilitates milk flow by contracting your milk ducts. Beyond its role in lactation, oxytocin stimulates uterine contractions, helping your uterus return to its pre-pregnancy size and reducing postpartum bleeding. The surge of oxytocin also strengthens the bond between you and the baby and supports your well-being by reducing stress and fostering a sense of calm.

However, if you’re not nursing, expect your oxytocin levels to return to normal within a couple of weeks. Likewise, when you start to wean, both prolactin and oxytocin levels will revert to normal

Diet plays a central role in supporting recovery, so we recommend a nutrient-rich diet to help you balance hormones after pregnancy. Discuss with your healthcare provider if there are foods to avoid while breastfeeding, but in general these are healthy:

Incorporating supplements to balance hormones after pregnancy, such as vitamin D, Magnesium, and Omega-3, can give you a much-needed boost if you cannot get the amounts you need through diet and lifestyle. Before taking any supplements, make sure to discuss them with your doctor, whether you are breastfeeding or not.

The timeline varies for each woman, but generally, it takes a few months for hormones to stabilize. After around six months, most women experience more stable hormone levels, with your body adapting to the demands of motherhood and hormonal fluctuations becoming less pronounced.

During these months, here are some ideas to help:

Returning to a regular menstrual cycle is often a sign that your hormones are starting to stabilize. Keep note of the frequency, duration, and any symptoms of your periods. Irregularities can be normal in the beginning, but tracking them can help identify patterns or issues that might need attention.

Maintain a journal of symptoms that could indicate hormonal fluctuations, such as mood changes, energy levels, sleep quality, and skin condition. This will help you recognize patterns over time and provide concrete data to discuss with your doctor.

Schedule regular check-ups with your healthcare provider to discuss your progress. These sessions can be invaluable for getting professional insights into your hormonal health, adjusting any necessary treatment plans, and getting reassurance or advice on the next steps.

By monitoring your progress, you gain insights into the effectiveness of your efforts to balance hormones after pregnancy. However, don’t despair if things are not improving.

At Stella Mattina, we understand that hormones are vital for your overall health and well-being and that any hormone imbalance can impact your body functions and quality of life. If after a few months your hormones are not getting back into balance, please don’t worry! Our women’s healthcare specialists understand the hormonal changes you’re going through after delivery of your baby. We can do a comprehensive hormone assessment and personalize a treatment for you with BioTe bioidentical hormone pellets to match your exact and unique needs.

So, to ensure you really enjoy these first months with your newborn, contact us today to find a doctor near you and let’s talk!

heading: How to Lose Weight with a Thyroid Disease
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Learn how to stay on track with weight loss goals and confidently manage both your thyroid disease and maintaining a sustainable weight.
tags: can thyroid nodules cause hormone imbalance; Hashimoto's diet for weight loss; lose weight with thyroid disease; understanding thyroid disease and weight gain
full_text:
Have you ever felt like your body is working against you when it comes to losing weight? If you have thyroid disease, this can be a frustrating reality – but the right strategies can help you take back control.

Thyroid diseases, especially conditions like hypothyroidism and Hashimoto’s, often lead to challenges like weight gain due to hormonal imbalances. In this post, we’ll explore practical strategies and dietary adjustments to help you lose weight while addressing the unique needs of your thyroid condition.

Weight gain due to thyroid problems typically occurs when your thyroid gland is underactive (hypothyroidism) and produces fewer hormones than your body needs.

These hormones – including T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine) – play a key role in regulating your body’s energy use and metabolic rate.

A sluggish thyroid slows down your body’s ability to burn calories, which can lead to weight gain, fatigue, and other symptoms.

For individuals with Hashimoto’s thyroiditis (an autoimmune condition where your body attacks your thyroid gland), the challenge can seem even more pronounced. You probably feel it’s a fight where you have a disadvantage and can’t win! But that’s not true.

Why?

Because Hashimoto’s weight gain often stems from inflammation and reduced hormone production. So – if you searched online for “how can I lose weight with an underactive thyroid?” or “how to lose weight with low thyroid,” knowing about this link to hormones and inflammation means you can start creating an effective weight-loss strategy.

Managing weight with thyroid disease requires a holistic approach that addresses both your thyroid function and overall health.

The strategies below can help restore balance and improve your metabolism.

The most important thing is to work with your doctor to optimize your thyroid medication dosage and regularly monitor your TSH, T3, and T4 levels to ensure they’re within the target range.

A well-planned thyroid diet to lose weight incorporates foods that support thyroid function while reducing inflammation.

Include nutrient-dense options such as fruits, vegetables, lean proteins, whole grains, and healthy fats, and avoid processed foods and added sugars, which can worsen your inflammation and disrupt your hormone balance.

Healthy fats (found in avocados, nuts, seeds, olive oil, and fish) are essential for hormone production. They reduce inflammation and support thyroid function.

Focus on having a proper balance of proteins, carbohydrates, and fats. This balance will

This is really important because weight loss can lead to reduced muscle mass, which brings you other problems you’d rather not have!

So, combine strength training and low-impact cardio to boost your metabolism and increase your muscle mass, with yoga and Pilates to reduce stress and improve flexibility.

For individuals with Hashimoto’s disease, weight management requires more than just diet and exercise.

To address both Hashimoto’s and weight gain you need to reduce inflammation, support your immune system, and adopt a holistic approach that integrates personalized medical care, nutrition, and lifestyle adjustments.

You can imagine that successfully addressing these issues requires a combination of targeted dietary strategies and lifestyle changes!

The Hashimoto’s autoimmune diet focuses on reducing inflammation, healing the gut, and optimizing thyroid health, with a focus on these foods:

1 Anti-inflammatory foods: Incorporate turmeric (rich in curcumin), leafy greens (packed with antioxidants and vitamins), and berries (high in fiber and antioxidants) to combat inflammation and support thyroid and immune health.

2 Iodine-rich foods: Iodine, found in seaweed, eggs, and iodized salt, is essential for thyroid hormone production. However, avoid excess intake, as it can exacerbate autoimmune issues.

3 Selenium-rich foods: Selenium, found in Brazil nuts and tuna, catalyzes the conversion of T4 into the active T3 hormone.

4 Gut-healing foods: Bone broth and fermented foods improve gut health, which is crucial for immune function and reduces inflammation.

In addition, to support thyroid health and manage inflammation, you should limit or avoid certain foods that can interfere with hormone balance and exacerbate your symptoms:

1 Trigger foods: Gluten and dairy are common triggers for autoimmune reactions and may worsen inflammation.

2 Goitrogens: Raw cruciferous vegetables, such as broccoli and kale, can interfere with iodine uptake. Cooking these vegetables minimizes their effects, making them safer in moderation.

3 Refined sugars and unhealthy fats: These contribute to inflammation and disrupt hormone balance.

Now, let’s answer two other questions we get asked…

If you’re preparing for thyroid blood work, fasting requirements vary depending on the test. Fasting is generally not required for TSH, T3, and T4 levels, but always confirm with your healthcare provider.

Certain medications, like thyroid hormone replacements, should be taken at a specific time before testing.

Thyroid nodules are abnormal growths in your thyroid gland that can sometimes lead to hormone imbalances.

Depending on whether the nodules are producing excess hormones or are inactive, they may cause symptoms like unexplained weight changes. If your healthcare provider suspects thyroid nodules, they will get them evaluated through an ultrasound or biopsy.

Quick and accurate results are essential for effective management when dealing with thyroid disease.

At Stella Mattina, you get a same-day appointment when necessary and also personalized care by working directly with your doctor.

With regular monitoring, we ensure your treatment plan is adjusted as necessary. Our aim is to help you stay on track with your weight loss goals so that you’re not only healthier but also more confident about managing your thyroid disease.

Schedule your appointment and let’s talk!

He arranges his schedule so that same-day appointments are usually available.

heading: How to Lose Weight With PCOS
author: Dr. Gonzalo Venegas
meta_description: Understanding how to lose weight with PCOS means no fad dieting but making steady progress over weeks and months – it's doable! Learn more.
tags: PCOS Diet; Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome Belly
full_text:
How to lose weight with PCOS (the usual way we refer to polycystic ovarian syndrome) is often challenging, but it’s definitely not impossible.

If you have a PCOS diagnosis, you’ve come to the right place to learn about how to lose weight with PCOS.

But first we need to briefly clarify two things:

The first is that you can have multiple ovarian cysts but not have PCOS. There’s a difference. And as we mentioned in our blog on ovarian cysts and birth control pills, they may cause bloating but not usually weight gain – although it can feel like it.

The second is that multiple ovarian cysts might be linked to PCOS sometimes. A polycystic ovarian syndrome specialist will be able to tell you how PCOS is diagnosed, how to get tested for PCOS, and offer a specific PCOS test – such as the PCOS ultrasound test.

But here, we’re going to look at how to lose weight with PCOS. Why is it so hard?

Weight loss with PCOS is affected by hormonal and metabolic factors that don’t apply in the same way to everyone else.

One of the most significant is insulin resistance, which is common in PCOS. When insulin levels are consistently high, the body is more likely to store fat and less likely to access stored fat for energy.

PCOS can also affect how your body regulates appetite and energy use.

Hormonal imbalances may increase hunger signals, reduce satiety after meals, and make blood sugar swings more pronounced.

This is why standard calorie-cutting approaches often lead to short-term results followed by plateaus or rapid regain.

Another issue is this: In PCOS, your body may burn fewer calories at rest than expected, particularly during aggressive dieting. That’s why restrictive diets are often ineffective over time if you have PCOS.

None of this means weight loss with PCOS isn’t achievable. You just need an effective strategy. One that reduces insulin resistance and stress levels over time.

You may have noticed that your extra weight gain is mostly around your abdomen. This pattern is closely linked to insulin resistance and cortisol (often referred to as the stress hormone).

When insulin levels remain elevated, fat storage is more likely to occur centrally than in your hips or thighs. Stress hormones can amplify this effect. Particularly if your sleep is poor or exercise intensity is too high for recovery.

This pattern can be frustrating.

But belly fat is responsive if you can improve your insulin sensitivity. For example, with resistance training, balanced meals with adequate protein and fiber, and consistent meal timing.

Targeting abdominal fat by addressing blood sugar regulation, stress load, and muscle preservation is what will bring you gradual but measurable change.

There isn’t a single prescribed PCOS diet. But there are clear nutritional patterns that support PCOS weight loss by improving blood sugar control and reducing insulin spikes. You’re aiming for stability.

Meals that help you lose weight with PCOS tend to combine fiber, protein, and healthy fats. This slows digestion, reduces blood sugar swings, and helps control your appetite across the day. It also helps your gut health.

Fiber-rich carbohydrates are a key part of this in appropriate portions.

Whole grains such as oatmeal, brown rice, and quinoa, along with beans, lentils, vegetables, and fruit, help regulate insulin response.

Protein is equally important.

Include protein at every meal – from foods such as fish, eggs, poultry, legumes, tofu, or Greek yogurt. This supports muscle mass and helps you feel full for longer.

Maintaining muscle is particularly important for PCOS weight loss. Why? Because muscle tissue improves insulin sensitivity.

Healthy fats also play a role.

Foods such as olive oil, nuts, seeds, and avocado support hormone function and slow absorption of carbs when eaten together in a meal.

Overall, diets that are plant-forward, minimally processed, and balanced tend to be easier to maintain and more effective for PCOS weight loss than highly restrictive plans.

You can start swapping these foods in gradually to make the change easier. And also swap some foods out…

Foods that cause rapid blood sugar spikes can make weight loss with PCOS more difficult. These include refined carbs, sugary drinks, sweets, and heavily processed snack foods.

Highly processed foods are often low in fiber and high in added sugars or refined starches. This can increase hunger shortly after eating.

Regularly relying on these foods can undermine your weight-loss efforts even when you’ve monitored calories.

Limiting these foods doesn’t mean complete avoidance. The focus is frequency and portion size, not perfection.

Skipping meals or eating erratically can worsen blood sugar instability. Regular meals with a balance of carbohydrates, protein, and fat tend to support more consistent energy levels and appetite control.

Food choices form the foundation of PCOS weight loss, but lifestyle factors influence how effectively those changes work. Here are two to bear in mind:

Exercise Can Support PCOS Weight Loss

Exercise is most effective for PCOS when it improves insulin sensitivity rather than simply burning calories.

Resistance training plays a central role here. Building and maintaining muscle helps your body use glucose more efficiently and supports metabolic health over time.

Consistent low-impact cardio such as walking, cycling, or swimming can also be helpful. These support fat loss without placing excessive stress on your body.

However, very high volumes of intense cardio are not always helpful for PCOS. In some cases, they can increase stress hormones and make appetite and fatigue harder to manage.

Sleep and Stress Management

Poor sleep and chronic stress can interfere with PCOS weight loss by increasing cortisol levels and disrupting appetite regulation.

Even small improvements in sleep duration or consistency can support better blood sugar control and reduce your cravings.

And finding ways to manage your stress levels can help support your weight loss efforts.

We’ve seen time and again in clinic that PCOS weight loss usually happens gradually – over months not weeks. Not via restrictive diets, but a balanced approach to regulating blood sugar and insulin with sustainable food choices.

Searching for best PCOS doctor in Dallas? If you’d like a PCOS specialist Dallas to support your weight loss, find our nearest location and make an appointment today.

Image attribution

Gonzalo Venegas, MD is the Medical Director of Stella Mattina Health and a respected obstetrician-gynecologist with over four decades of service in the Dallas medical community. He has led the Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology at Methodist Dallas Medical Center and contributed to community health through his role on the Parkland Health & Hospital System Board. A dedicated mentor at UT Southwestern, Dr. Venegas is known for his leadership, compassion, and commitment to advancing women’s health and medical education.

heading: How to Manage Common Pregnancy Concerns
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Learn the answers to all your common pregnancy concerns and put your mind at rest so you can enjoy the full nine months!
tags: challenging symptoms during pregnancy; common pregnancy concerns; concerns about weight gain during pregnancy; diet during pregnancy
full_text:
Common pregnancy concerns will obviously vary according to whether it’s your first baby or a later pregnancy, but they’re likely to include questions about your diet, weight, symptoms, and general health. So – let’s dive in to the ones we get asked about most.

“I’m worried about getting enough nutrients for my baby’s health, but I’m also trying to avoid harmful foods. It’s a bit overwhelming trying to eat healthily, and I’m always wondering if I’m doing it right.”

The thought of harming your baby is scary, especially during a first pregnancy. So, one of the most common pregnancy concerns is about food to avoid.

Fish: Avoid raw shellfish; limit tuna because it has more mercury in it; avoid cold-smoked or cured fish that can have listeria bacteria; and limit oily fish like salmon and mackerel to two portions a week – they may contain pollutants, but you do need omega-3 fatty acids for your baby’s brain development.

Dairy: Avoid anything made from unpasteurized milk. The reason, again, is possible listeria contamination.

The nutritional expert at your women’s health center will discuss other items to avoid that you usually enjoy.

Alcohol: The is no evidenced “safe” amount – some passes through the placenta to your baby. It is best to refrain from any alcohol.

A varied diet will give you enough vitamins and minerals, especially folic acid, iron, calcium, and DHA. Folic acid is important during the first 28 days, so try to increase your intake to recommended amounts before you start a pregnancy. Both folic acid and fish oils can be in the form of supplements.

Vegetables and fruit provide vitamins and minerals. You’ll get iron and folic acid from grains (cereals). And, if your diet allows for animal products, dairy items will give you calcium, potassium, and vitamins A and D. You can get plenty of protein from beans, peas, nuts, seeds, and meat.

If you’re vegetarian or vegan, however, speak to a clinical nutritionist to ensure a nutritional balance.

The best way to manage food aversions or cravings is to concentrate on getting the nutrition you need from your overall diet.

You can also disguise a disliked item in another preferred choice! Or hide a craved food you know will unbalance your diet. When you don’t feel like eating, try “little and often” rather than full meals.

Keep water near you during the day to avoid dehydration. You and your fetus need around 8-10 glasses of water a day to provide for

Another common pregnancy concern is caffeine in cola, coffee, tea, and energy drinks. The recommendation is to limit caffeine to less than 200 mg a day, or about 2 cups of coffee.

You’ll gain weight but want to shed any extra weight afterwards! Expect to gain no more than 25 lbs. This depends on whether you’re overweight or underweight beforehand – and also whether you’re having more than one baby – then, up to 40 lbs is expected.

If you attend for regular prenatal care, your doctor will monitor this. However, if you think in terms of 2-4 lbs in the first trimester and one pound a week thereafter, you can feel relaxed about weight gain.

Remember, too, why you’re putting on weight: It includes your enlarging uterus, a growing baby, amniotic fluid and placenta, extra blood supply, fat stores, and breast tissue!

“The morning sickness has been tough, and I’m unsure if it’s normal or something to worry about. And then there’s the fatigue; I never knew I could be this tired.”

Morning sickness: Try to eat in small amounts while this lasts (it can happen at any time of day!) – avoiding fats and choosing higher carbs. Sip your drinks slowly if you feel sick.

Fatigue: You feel more tired because you’re growing a baby! So – rest when you need to. And limit extra activity while you’re at your most tired – usually the first 12 weeks due to hormone changes, and the last few weeks as you grow bigger. Ask for help, and remember that, paradoxically, exercise can help you re-energize!

Back pain and body aches: Again, some physical activity can help, as can sleeping on your side to relieve back pain. Your ligaments are stretching, so avoid unnecessary strain while lifting or bending. Stretching exercises or yoga can be helpful.

“Finding a comfortable sleeping position is difficult now my belly’s getting bigger. I toss and turn and worry if I’m accidentally sleeping in a position that isn’t safe for the baby.

Sleep: This is a common pregnancy concern. You’ll feel extra tired at times, so you need more sleep. Your body is under pressure to support your growing baby, and sleep restores your energy and bodily systems. Find whatever position helps you sleep comfortably, even upright at times.

Exercise: Managing the right amount of exercise isn’t a spreadsheet matter, but aim for 150 minutes of aerobic exercise a week to keep your muscles healthy. You’ll know if you overexert yourself. Keep your heart rate less than 140 bpm and increase water intake.

Emotional health: Women’s healthcare specialists understand about managing the emotional changes you’re undergoing, so talk to them. Also, do something enjoyable each day, use whatever stress-busting techniques work for you, and maybe keep a journal to help you make sense of your pregnancy feelings.

If you’re worried about any of the following issues, your ObGyn specialist can advise you how best to manage:

At Stella Mattina, our expert women’s healthcare specialists understand these common pregnancy concerns and can put your mind at rest. We focus on personalized, compassionate care for all women and encourage you to access regular prenatal care so you can enjoy the full nine months of your pregnancy.

Contact us today and let’s talk!

heading: How to Prevent STDs – Best Practices and Strategies
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Learn how to prevent STDs in our step-by-step guide – and why early detection via regular testing is the best route to successful treatment.
tags: how to prevent STDs; what are STDs?; why prevention of STDs matters
full_text:
Sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) affect millions of people each year in the US, causing emotional stress, straining relationships, and impacting overall well-being. To stay healthy, it’s essential you know how to prevent STDs – and the good news is that they are largely preventable.

In recognition of World AIDS Day 2024 on December 1st, we’ll run through the most important facts in this article so you can protect your health and make informed decisions.

STDs are infections spread primarily through sexual contact. They can result from bacteria (e.g., chlamydia), viruses (e.g., HIV, herpes), or parasites (e.g., trichomoniasis). If left untreated, STDs can lead to serious health complications, including infertility, chronic pain, and increased vulnerability to other infections.

Fortunately, there are many ways to prevent STDs, and understanding how they’re transmitted is the first step toward protecting your health. We’ll look at that in a moment, but first, why does prevention matter if they’re mostly curable if caught early?

Preventing STDs is not just about protecting yourself from physical harm. It’s also about protecting your emotional well-being and reducing the broader public health burden.

So – here’s a breakdown of why prevention is so important:

Protecting your health: While many STDs are treatable, some – like HIV and herpes – are lifelong conditions that require ongoing management. Other infections, such as untreated syphilis or gonorrhea, can cause severe complications.

Breaking the cycle of silent transmission: Many STDs are asymptomatic, meaning individuals may carry and transmit the infection without knowing it. Regular testing and preventive measures help break this cycle.

Addressing stigma: Open conversations about sexual health can reduce the stigma surrounding STDs. If we can talk about prevention, testing, and treatment openly and without judgment, we create an environment where seeking help feels safe and accessible.

Combatting rising rates: Empowering individuals with knowledge and access to resources can help reverse this trend and improve public health outcomes.

To effectively prevent STDs, you need a proactive, informed approach. Here’s how you can protect yourself and your partners:

Knowledge is your first line of defense. Learn how STDs are transmitted, what symptoms may appear (if any), and the risks associated with different sexual activities.

Can condoms prevent all STDs? Unfortunately, no. While condoms are highly effective at reducing STD risk when used properly, they cannot prevent all STDs. They do offer protection against infections spread through bodily fluids, such as HIV and chlamydia. They are less effective, though still helpful, against infections transmitted through skin-to-skin contact, like HPV and herpes.

Testing is a cornerstone of STD prevention. Even if you feel healthy, you could still be carrying an infection. Regular screenings help detect and treat infections early. This reduces complications and stops the spread.

When to test:

What testing provides:

Having conversations about sexual health and protection with your partners builds trust. Here’s how to approach the topic:

Vaccination can prevent certain STDs:

HPV vaccine: Protects against several strains of the human papillomavirus, including those that cause most genital warts and cervical cancers.

Hepatitis B vaccine: Prevents hepatitis B, a virus that can cause liver damage and cancer.

Making intentional choices about your sexual activity can reduce your risk of contracting STDs. Consider these tips:

Limit your partners: Reducing your number of sexual partners lowers your chances of exposure.

Avoid substance use before sex: Alcohol and drugs can impair your judgment, leading to unprotected sex or other risky behaviors. Yes, it’s easily done, but try to avoid taking extra risks!

Consider mutual monogamy: Being in a committed relationship with a partner who has tested negative for STDs can significantly reduce your risk.

Can birth control prevent STDs? No. While excellent for preventing pregnancy, birth control does not prevent STDs. Always pair hormonal birth control with condoms or other barrier methods for comprehensive protection.

Yes, this is part of preventing STDs. If you take what you’ve learned and share it with others, you help foster a community of informed individuals. Here’s what you can do to help:

Taking control of your sexual health is empowering, and it starts with education and action. By implementing the strategies outlined here, you can significantly reduce your risk and encourage others to do the same.

Let World AIDS Day 2024 inspire us to have open conversations about sexual health, challenge stigma, and commit to proactive STD prevention!

And who knows? By World Aids Day next year, you’ll perhaps not be able to name anyone in your circle who’s had an STD in the last year.

At Stella Mattina, we’re at the forefront of women’s health and passionate about your well-being.

All our doctors are easy to talk to and all are knowledgeable about STDs, having dealt with hundreds of patient over the years. They will not embarrass or shame you.

Early detection is the best route to successful treatment. If you’re concerned about a possible infection and want an STD test, book your appointment now while it’s in your mind.

heading: How We Support Expecting Mothers from Day One
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Our OBGYNs in Dallas are here for all expecting mothers, offering you expert help from fertility support through to postpartum care.
tags: blood work during pregnancy; expecting mothers; fertility support; first prenatal appointment
full_text:
As an expecting mother you want and deserve comprehensive, compassionate support – from pre-pregnancy planning to postpartum recovery.

In this article, we’ll cover all the most important questions, starting with how we can help you to get that positive test – and including:

Let’s jump straight in!

Before that “life-changing” pregnancy test turns positive, there are critical steps that can make a significant difference in your journey to becoming a mother.

If you’re trying to conceive, and success seems elusive, we provide resources and support for you. We know it can be frustrating! So, please come in for lifestyle guidance, prenatal supplements, and fertility consultations.

Why have a fertility consultation? Because fertility tests can offer valuable insight into your ovulation patterns, egg quality, and overall fertility. This helps you understand your body and optimize your chances of conception.

Once you see those two lines or a glowing “pregnant” on the test, it’s natural to feel a rush of emotions. The first step for all expectant mothers is to schedule an initial prenatal appointment with your healthcare provider.

At your first prenatal appointment, your healthcare provider will confirm the pregnancy, calculate your due date, and begin building a comprehensive care plan tailored to your unique needs.

This initial visit involves your first lab test for pregnancy – to confirm the pregnancy and check your hormone levels, including hCG, to ensure everything is progressing normally.

It’s a reasonable question to want to know what it’s “for” – especially if you’re not keen on blood draws. But blood work is a vital part of prenatal care. It provides essential information about your health and the health of your baby.

For expecting mothers, these tests can detect potential issues early, guide your personalized care plan, and offer you peace of mind at an emotional time.

They’re used for:

If you’re searching for blood work for pregnancy near me, we have same-day appointments to suit your schedule.

Your first prenatal appointment is a comprehensive visit designed to establish a strong foundation for your pregnancy care. These are the things your healthcare provider will do:

Review your medical history, which includes any previous pregnancies, existing medical conditions, and your family’s health history.

Perform a physical exam, including a pelvic exam, to assess the size of your uterus and confirm the pregnancy.

Conduct ultrasounds: Depending on how far along you are, you might have your first ultrasound to check the baby’s heartbeat and confirm your due date.

Order blood work for pregnancy: As mentioned earlier, this step is critical for assessing your overall health and detecting any potential concerns early.

Discuss prenatal vitamins and nutrition: Your provider will guide you on the best nutrients to support your baby’s growth, including folic acid, iron, and calcium.

Create a personalized prenatal appointment schedule to ensure regular check-ins throughout your pregnancy. This allows for monitoring of both your health and your baby’s development.

After your initial prenatal appointment, your provider will establish a regular schedule of check-ups to monitor your baby’s growth and your overall well-being. A typical schedule might look like this:

Weeks 4-28: Monthly visits Weeks 28-36: Bi-weekly visits Weeks 36-Birth: Weekly visits

However, recent, new guidance suggests a more personalized approach where appropriate, with possibly fewer in-person visits and other care modalities.

At Stella Mattina, we go beyond just routine prenatal care services. We understand that every pregnancy is unique, so you need comprehensive, personalized care that includes a number of things:

Personalized care plans that evolve with your pregnancy, guided by specialists who take time to understand your unique needs and preferences.

Advanced monitoring, from high-resolution ultrasounds to ongoing fetal health checks, ensures early detection of concerns and continuous peace of mind.

Genetic testing and counseling to help you understand hereditary risks and make informed decisions.

High-risk pregnancy management, with maternal fetal medicine specialists trained to handle every complication like gestational diabetes or preeclampsia.

Support for good nutrition, emotional health, and fitness.

As your pregnancy progresses, your care needs will evolve.

This includes more frequent prenatal appointments, additional blood work for pregnant women, and specialized screenings, such as glucose tolerance tests, to monitor for gestational diabetes.

We ensure that no detail is overlooked, so you can have peace of mind from the first positive test through delivery and beyond.

As your due date approaches, you’ll likely have questions about birth plans, hospital tours, and what to expect in those first precious moments with your newborn.

Don’t worry about it! We’ll guide you every step of the way.

And after childbirth, we’ll continue to support you with postpartum check-ups, lactation consulting, and personalized guidance to help you navigate breastfeeding or bottle feeding, full physical and hormonal recovery, and any challenges that may arise.

Choosing the right prenatal healthcare provider is one of the most important decisions you’ll make as a parent.

At Stella Mattina, we specialize in providing expert care focused on the needs of all women. That obviously includes you when you’re pregnant.

From fertility support to postpartum care, our experienced ObGyns in Dallas are here for you – providing the expertise you need to navigate your pregnancy with enjoyment.

Choose your most convenient Stella Mattina location and call in!

Providing a full range of obstetrical and gynecological care, he specializes in the treatment of endometriosis and robotic surgery. heading: Infertility Signs and Symptoms in Women – How to Know if You’re Infertile
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Conversations tend to avoid discussing symptoms and signs of infertility. Here's everything you need to know to help you towards conceiving.
tags: diagnosing infertility; signs of infertility; treatment options for infertility; what causes infertility; what is infertility
full_text:
Infertility is a deeply personal and often misunderstood health issue that affects millions of women worldwide. While it’s common to hear about pregnancy success stories, conversations around the challenges of conceiving can be quietly suppressed. Even friends tend to avoid talk about signs of infertility. This can leave you isolated and filled with uncertainty.

But infertility doesn’t need to be taboo. Knowledge empowers you. Understanding the early symptoms and signs of infertility can be the first step toward infertile couples getting the support and treatment they need.

With that in mind, we’ve compiled answers to the infertility FAQs we hear in the clinics. So, keep reading if you wish to explore what infertility means, how it’s diagnosed, and why early awareness matters.

We’ll discuss the most common signs of infertility and potential causes, their emotional and psychological impacts, and the options available. Whether you’re trying to conceive now or planning for the future, here is the information you need.

Table of Contents What Is Infertility, and When Should You Be Concerned? What Causes Female Infertility? What Are the Signs of Infertility in a Woman? How is Infertility Diagnosed? What Are the Treatment Options for Female Infertility?

Infertility is often defined as the inability to conceive after 12 months of regular, unprotected sex (or 6 months for women over 35).

But beyond this medical definition lies a complex and emotional experience. So, it’s important to understand what infertility means, and how it differs from other reproductive challenges like sterility.

Unlike sterility, which refers to a complete inability to conceive often due to a medical condition or surgical procedure, infertility doesn’t necessarily mean pregnancy is impossible. It may just be taking longer than expected or require medical assistance.

So, there’s no need to fret, because many women with infertility eventually conceive with the right treatment or support.

Please talk to your doctor as soon as possible if you’ve been trying to conceive for over a year (or six months if you’re 35 or older) without success, They can then identify potential issues sooner and explore your treatment options. This will help you avoid the emotional toll of prolonged uncertainty.

But what are the possible root causes of female infertility? We’ll look at these causes first, and then in a moment look at possible signs of infertility you might notice.

Let’s now look at the most common causes of female infertility:

These are among the most common causes of infertility in women.

If you’re not ovulating regularly, or at all, it becomes difficult to conceive. Certain conditions can interfere with the hormonal balance needed to avoid ovulation disorders. These include polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), thyroid disorders, high prolactin levels, or even extreme stress or weight fluctuations.

So, it’s crucial to address these underlying conditions when you’re trying to conceive to increase your chances of regular ovulation.

Your fallopian tubes are essential for fertilization – your eggs travel this path to meet the sperm.

If your fallopian tubes have a blockage due to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), previous infections, endometriosis, or past surgeries, your egg and the sperm may never meet. This makes conception impossible without medical intervention.

Endometriosis is another common cause of infertility in women.

This condition occurs when tissue similar to the uterine lining grows outside the uterus – often on your ovaries, fallopian tubes, or other pelvic structures. This can cause scarring, inflammation, and pain. And it may also interfere with egg release, fertilization, or implantation.

Now, you may be asking yourself if all women with endometriosis are infertile. And, fortunately, the answer is no.

What percent of infertile women have endometriosis?

Among all women with endometriosis, 30–50% may experience difficulties getting pregnant. So, even if you were diagnosed with this condition, you still have the chance of getting pregnant with the right treatment.

Structural issues within the uterus, such as polyps, fibroids, or a septate uterus, can make it difficult for an embryo to implant and grow. In some cases, abnormalities in your cervical mucus or your cervix can also prevent sperm from traveling effectively.

This occurs when ovaries stop functioning normally before age 40. It can happen due to genetic conditions, autoimmune diseases, or sometimes for unknown reasons. Women with this condition may experience irregular periods and low estrogen levels, all of which can affect fertility.

Infertility can sometimes be silent, but in many cases, your body offers subtle (or not-so-subtle) clues that something may be interfering with conception.

But why is early detection and awareness important? There are several reasons. One is that damage to your reproductive organs can get worse and may become untreatable. Another is that as you get older, your chances of conception (like those of most women) become less likely.

So – being aware of the following signs of infertility can help prompt you to get earlier medical evaluation and support so you conceive as soon as possible.

Here are some of the most common signs of infertility that you may experience:

Cycles that are consistently shorter than 21 days or longer than 35 days, or that vary significantly from month to month, can indicate ovulation issues. Ovulation is key to conception, and abnormal periods often signal that it’s not occurring regularly.

Do infertile women have periods?

Yes, many women who struggle with infertility continue to have a menstrual cycle. In fact, this can be one of the most confusing signs of infertility. You may feel like your body is working just fine because your period shows up each month, but something deeper could still be interfering with your ability to conceive.

In short, having periods doesn’t guarantee fertility, and missing periods doesn’t necessarily mean you’re infertile. That’s why fertility evaluations are so important, as they help uncover what’s happening beneath the surface.

Missing periods for several months (when not pregnant) could be a sign of underlying hormonal imbalances, stress, excessive exercise, or conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), all of which can affect fertility.

Menstrual pain is common, but severe cramping or unusually heavy bleeding in every menstrual cycle might be a sign of endometriosis or fibroids. These are both health conditions that can interfere with your ability to conceive.

Symptoms like unexplained weight gain, thinning hair, excessive facial or body hair, or persistent acne may signal hormonal imbalances related to polycystic ovarian syndrome or thyroid dysfunction. Both can disrupt ovulation and fertility.

Sex should not be painful, so painful sex (dyspareunia) may be one of the warning signs of infertility: It could be linked to conditions like endometriosis or pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), which may affect fertility.

Experiencing two or more consecutive miscarriages may suggest an underlying fertility issue. Causes can range from genetic abnormalities and uterine conditions to immune or hormonal problems.

Autoimmune disorders, thyroid problems, diabetes, hormonal imbalances, and previous pelvic surgeries or infections can all play a role in fertility challenges.

As mentioned, quite often one of the biggest signs of infertility is simply the absence of conception despite regular, unprotected intercourse for 12 months (or 6 months if over age 35). This is the clinical threshold for seeking fertility evaluation.

Just to recap, here are the red flags you should not ignore:

If you’ve been trying to get pregnant without success, it’s natural to feel confused, anxious, or even frustrated. But you have options.

So, if you experience any of the signs of infertility mentioned above, talk to your doctor immediately. They will help to diagnose infertility and guide you through the best treatment options.

After all, getting a clear infertility diagnosis is the first step to finding the right path forward!

So, here’s what you can expect from a thorough fertility evaluation and the diagnosis procedure:

Your healthcare provider will start by reviewing information about your menstrual cycle, past pregnancies (if any), any history of miscarriages or ectopic pregnancy, and general health. In addition, they may do a pelvic exam to check for signs of anatomical or hormonal abnormalities.

This is also your chance to ask questions or bring information you want to share.

So– prepare a list over a few months to take with you to your appointment and remember to include:

Your doctor will request a range of blood tests to evaluate reproductive hormones involved in ovulation and female fertility, including:

These markers indicate whether your body is ovulating regularly and whether your egg supply is within a healthy range.

A transvaginal ultrasound provides a closer look at your uterus and ovaries. It can detect issues like ovarian cysts, uterine fibroids, or a thin uterine lining. It’s also used to monitor follicle development during the ovulation cycle.

An HSG is a specialized X-ray used to check whether your fallopian tubes are open and functioning properly. During the procedure, your healthcare provider will inject a contrast dye into your uterus and take images to see if the dye flows freely through the tubes.

Your ovarian reserve refers to the number and quality of your remaining eggs, which naturally decrease as you get older. The AMH test – along with other hormone levels and ultrasound assessments (like antral follicle count) – helps evaluate your reproductive potential.

If other tests don’t reveal the cause of infertility, or if there’s a suspicion of endometriosis, adhesions, or pelvic scarring, your doctor may recommend a laparoscopy. This is a minimally invasive surgical procedure that allows a close look inside the pelvis using a small camera.

Now, let’s get to what really matters: fertility treatments and how to increase your chances of getting pregnant.

The good news is that many causes of infertility are treatable. And with the right support, you can likely conceive successfully.

The best fertility treatments, however, will depend on the underlying cause of your infertility, your age, your overall health, and how long you’ve been trying to get pregnant. Some of the most common and effective treatment options available include the following:

If you don’t ovulate regularly, fertility medications can help stimulate ovulation. The most commonly used drugs include:

These infertility treatments encourage your ovaries to produce and release eggs, increasing your chances of conception – either naturally or in combination with other treatments.

When simpler approaches don’t resolve signs of infertility and lead to pregnancy, your healthcare provider may recommend some of the following assisted reproductive technologies for your infertility treatment:

These methods can be powerful solutions, especially when combined with medical support. and lifestyle adjustments. Let’s look at both of those now.

In some cases, surgical treatment may be necessary to improve fertility, especially if physical blockages or abnormalities are affecting your uterus, fallopian tubes, or ovaries. Common procedures include:

Surgical treatment can restore normal anatomy, improve ovulation, and enhance your chances of natural conception.

In addition to medical treatments that can positively affect fertility, some simple lifestyle changes can also improve your fertility. Some strategies include:

Infertility can be emotionally and physically overwhelming for infertile couples, but it’s not a dead-end street, and you’re not alone. We’ve shown you the likely causes, symptoms, and possibilities for helping you conceive via treatment or lifestyle changes.

The next step is to be proactive NOW and seek help without delay!

At Stella Mattina, we offer advanced fertility testing in Dallas. You can talk with a compassionate gynecologist/fertility specialist and take the first step toward infertility treatment and planned motherhood.

Don’t navigate infertility alone! Schedule your fertility consultation today and get the care you deserve.

heading: Iron Deficiency and Anemia: What’s the Difference?
author: Dr. Gonzalo Venegas
meta_description: Iron deficiency and anemia are linked but not the same. You can have iron deficiency without anemia – but not the reverse. Learn more!
tags: best foods to eat for iron deficiency; can men have iron deficiency; iron deficiency vs. anemia; symptoms of iron deficiency
full_text:
Iron deficiency means your body doesn’t have enough iron stored. Anemia means you don’t have enough healthy red blood cells to carry oxygen.

Iron deficiency is one of the most common causes of anemia, but you can have iron deficiency without anemia.

On the other hand, you can’t have anemia caused by low iron unless iron deficiency is already present.

Because fatigue, headaches, or dizziness can come from either condition, knowing the difference helps you decide when it’s time to see a doctor. (If you’ve already decided you need help today, take advantage of our same-day appointments.)

Now, let’s have a closer look at how iron deficiency is different from anemia. And then we’ll answer questions we get asked.

For example, whether men can have iron deficiency, what deficiency symptoms look like, some good foods to eat that contain iron, and about consulting with your doctor in case you need treatment.

Iron deficiency happens when your iron stores run low. Iron is essential for making hemoglobin, the protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen throughout your body.

Anemia is the condition that results when you don’t have enough healthy red blood cells. It has several possible causes: vitamin deficiencies, chronic disease, or blood loss. But iron deficiency is the most common.

You can have iron deficiency without anemia. This means your iron stores are too low, but your red blood cell count is still within range.

However, over time, if you leave your iron deficiency untreated, you can progress to being anemic.

Yes, they can. Many people think of iron deficiency as a women’s health issue, because they hear about it due to menstruation or pregnancy. But both men and women can suffer a deficiency. Men and iron deficiency is an important topic.

So, can a man have iron deficiency? Absolutely. In men, causes can include:

That’s why unexplained fatigue or weakness in men should always be checked out by a doctor. Men sometimes delay seeking care, but if the man in your life seems to suffer fatigue or weakness without reason, encourage them to seek help!

Here are some more symptoms to look out for in cases of iron deficiency…

Iron deficiency symptoms can appear slowly and may overlap with many other conditions. However, common signs include:

Some symptoms raise specific questions:

If these symptoms persist, it’s best to have blood tests done. There are treatments to solve this deficiency, so waiting is pointless when you could start feeling well again.

Your doctor may suggest eating more iron-rich foods. Here are a few examples…

Diet plays an important role in prevention and recovery. Some of the best foods to eat for iron deficiency include:

For vegetarians and vegans: Iron is still very achievable without animal products – but it’s all non-heme iron, which your body absorbs less easily. To help:

These small adjustments can make a big difference in maintaining healthy iron levels. Please don’t self-dose iron pills without testing, because this is not safe medically.

If you’re looking for a doctor for anemia, your first step should be your primary care provider. They can run blood tests, identify if you have iron deficiency, and start treatment.

Treatment for anemia may include dietary changes as mentioned above, oral iron supplements, or (in some cases) IV iron. The best plan depends on the cause.

Sometimes your provider will refer you to iron deficiency specialists, such as:

Patients often search for the best anemia doctors near me, but the best starting point is your primary care doctor, who can direct you to the right specialist if needed.

See your doctor if you have:

Remember, both men and women can develop iron deficiency and anemia. Getting tested early makes treatment easier and prevents complications.

At Stella clinics, our primary care teams help men and women every day with testing, diagnosis, and treatment plans.

Iron deficiency and anemia are common, treatable, and best managed early.

If you’re wondering whether your symptoms point to iron deficiency or anemia, our Stella Mattina clinics are here to help – with experienced primary care providers who see both men and women every day.

We serve many locations in Dallas-Fort Worth, and you can access primary care at all of them. We also have Specialist Primary Care available at Carrollton, Dallas North, and Buckner.

With same-day appointments and drop-ins, access is easy and fits around your lifestyle. Searching for primary care in Dallas? Choose your nearest location or call us on 214-942-3100.

A dedicated mentor at UT Southwestern, Dr. Venegas is known for his leadership, compassion, and commitment to advancing women’s health and medical education.
