heading: What Causes Ovarian Cysts and How Birth Control Pills Can Affect Them
author: Dr. Gonzalo Venegas
meta_description: Clarify how ovarian cysts and birth control are related and find out what’s typical, what’s not, and when treatment is needed.
tags: can birth control pills affect ovarian cysts; ovarian cyst treatment options; ovarian cysts
full_text:
Ovarian cysts most often develop as part of normal hormone changes during the menstrual cycle, particularly around ovulation. Birth control pills can influence whether some cysts form by changing how the ovaries function, but their role depends on the type of cyst and the person’s symptoms.

Understanding what’s typical, what’s not, and when treatment is needed will help you clarify how ovarian cysts and birth control are related.

Let’s start with what exactly they are.

Cysts of this kind are fluid-filled sacs that form on or inside your ovary, and they’re very common during the reproductive years.

In many cases, they develop naturally as your ovaries prepare to release an egg – and resolve without causing symptoms.

Because cysts can form for different reasons and behave in different ways, doctors look at timing, size, and symptoms before deciding whether you need any follow-up or treatment.

Most ovarian cysts form as part of the normal ovulation process.

Each month, your ovaries prepare and release an egg. During that cycle, a small sac can develop and sometimes continue to fill with fluid instead of breaking down as expected.

Hormonal shifts, timing within your menstrual cycle, and how your ovary responds to those hormones all play a role in what causes ovarian cysts to form in the first place.

However, not all cysts are related to ovulation. Some develop independently of your menstrual cycle and behave differently over time.

This is why clinicians focus on how a cyst looks, how long it’s been present, and whether symptoms are changing, rather than assuming all cysts follow the same pattern.

The relationship between ovarian cysts and the pill comes down to ovulation.

Combined hormonal birth control pills work by preventing ovulation, which reduces the chance of forming the most common type of functional cyst.

This means the pill can lower the likelihood of new cysts developing – rather than acting as a treatment for cysts that already exist.

Because not all ovarian cysts are caused by ovulation, however, birth control does not affect every cyst in the same way.

Whether the pill is helpful depends on the type of cyst, your symptom history, and your overall reproductive health. That’s why treatment decisions are individualized.

You may not have any symptoms at all. Many ovarian cysts only come to light during routine exams or imaging done for another reason. But for other people, symptoms can vary widely.

They’re often related to pressure or irritation in the pelvis rather than the cyst itself being dangerous.

Common cyst-on-ovary symptoms include pelvic discomfort, bloating, a feeling of fullness, pain on one side of the lower abdomen, or discomfort during sex.

Changes in symptoms matter more than their presence alone.

So, take note of worsening pain, new sensations that don’t follow your usual cycle pattern, or symptoms that interfere with daily life.

These are typically what prompt further evaluation, rather than mild or occasional discomfort on its own.

While most ovarian cysts are harmless, some situations need prompt medical attention. An ovarian cyst rupture can cause sudden, sharp pelvic pain, sometimes accompanied by nausea, vomiting, dizziness, or fever.

This kind of pain is typically different from the dull or intermittent discomfort many people notice with uncomplicated cysts.

Size can also matter, but it isn’t the only factor doctors consider.

There’s no single cutoff that defines what size of ovarian cyst is dangerous. Symptoms, appearance, and how quickly a cyst changes are just as important as measurements.

For example, a smaller cyst causing severe pain may be more concerning than a larger one that remains stable and symptom-free.

The most common diagnosing tool is imaging, particularly an ultrasound on ovarian cysts. This test allows doctors to see the cyst’s size, shape, and internal features – which helps distinguish between cyst types and guides next steps.

Doctors can also use this non-invasive ultrasound of ovaries for initial diagnosis and follow-up to see whether a cyst resolves on its own.

They interpret imaging results alongside your symptoms, medical history, and where you are in your menstrual cycle.

This combination helps determine whether monitoring is best or whether treatment might be appropriate.

Ovarian cyst treatment depends on several factors, including symptoms, cyst appearance on imaging, age, and whether the cyst changes over time.

In many cases, you don’t need immediate treatment. Functional cysts often resolve on their own, and follow-up imaging can confirm if they’re shrinking or disappearing.

When symptoms are present, treatment may focus on pain management and monitoring rather than intervention.

Surgery is typically reserved for cysts that persist, grow, cause significant symptoms, or appear suspicious on imaging.

The goal is always to match the level of treatment to your level of risk, rather than treating every cyst the same way.

Birth control pills for ovarian cysts are most often used to help prevent new functional cysts from forming, not to treat existing ones.

By suppressing ovulation, hormonal birth control reduces the chance that the ovary will form the type of cyst most commonly linked to the menstrual cycle.

This approach isn’t necessary or helpful for everyone.

Because some ovarian cysts are not related to ovulation, doctors usually base decisions about using hormonal contraception on symptom patterns, recurrence, and your overall reproductive health, rather than the presence of a single cyst.

It’s a good idea to talk with a doctor if ovarian cyst symptoms persist, worsen, or keep returning over time.

Ongoing pelvic pain, changes in your menstrual cycle, pain during sex, or symptoms that interfere with daily life are all reasons to seek care rather than wait things out. Even when cysts are common and often harmless, patterns over time matter.

An OBGYN can help determine whether monitoring is enough or whether further evaluation makes sense – especially if imaging shows changes or symptoms don’t line up with a typical cycle.

If you’re Googling for an OBGYN Dallas provider or an OBGYN Dallas TX clinic, choosing one who offers continuity of care makes it easier to track changes and make informed decisions if cysts recur.

Check out our locations near you and book with one of our OBGYNs for both continuity of care and peace of mind.

heading: What is Allergy Season? Key Facts and Best Medicines
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: You don't have to suffer allergy season every year! Learn what it is and discover five ways you can effectively avoid or relieve symptoms.
tags: allergy season; best medicine for allergy season; hayfever; pollen allergy; seasonal allergies vs. common cold; when is peak allergy season?
full_text:
Allergy season is a period when many people experience symptoms due to increased pollen in the air. It can turn a beautiful spring day into a sneezy, congested ordeal. You find yourself dreading certain times of year because of constant sneezing, runny nose, or itchy eyes.

You’re not alone! In fact, the CDC reports that roughly one quarter of American adults suffer from seasonal allergies. The good news is that understanding what they are, when they occur, and how to manage can help you get relief.

Allergy season doesn’t refer to a single fixed date – it depends on where you live and what you’re allergic to. Generally, however, seasonal allergies flare up when certain plants pollinate.

In many regions, there are three main pollen seasons each year, corresponding to tree, grass, and weed pollens

Keep in mind that these seasons can overlap, and the exact timing varies by climate. In warmer southern regions or tropical climates, some plants pollinate early or even year-round.

This depends on regional plant life. Generally, tree pollen counts often rise in March and April, grass pollen tends to peak in late spring through summer (May to July), and weed pollen peaks late summer into fall (August-October).

However, in southern states, cedar pollen significantly contributes to extreme seasonal allergies, notably from December through February.

The intensity of seasonal allergies can fluctuate. They usually worsen on dry, windy days when pollen disperses more effectively. Conversely, symptoms often diminish during wet, rainy conditions, as pollen becomes grounded. Peak pollen times, typically early morning through midday, also intensify symptoms.

It’s easy to confuse a bad case of seasonal allergies with a common cold, since both can cause sneezing, congestion, and a runny nose. But there are some differences to help you figure out what’s going on:

While many people have mild to moderate sniffles or sneezes, others can experience intense symptoms that significantly impact daily life, such as severe nasal congestion and sinus pressure, violent coughing, intense eye itching or tearing – and even sinus infections, ear infections, or asthma flare-ups.

This begs the question: How can you avoid symptoms during allergy season?

The best medicine for allergy season depends on your symptoms. But often a combination of treatments is most effective. The goal is to block or reduce your body’s allergic reaction and relieve your discomfort.

Antihistamines block histamine, the chemical your immune system releases during an allergic reaction.

They help relieve sneezing, runny nose, and itchiness (eyes, nose, throat) and are available over-the-counter as pills (e.g., loratadine, cetirizine, fexofenadine) and as eyedrops or nasal sprays.

Nasal corticosteroid sprays are often the most effective treatment for nasal allergy symptoms. Steroid nasal sprays (like fluticasone, budesonide, or triamcinolone) reduce inflammation in the nasal passages.

They relieve congestion, sneezing, and nasal irritation, and are especially helpful for moderate to severe hay fever. Nasal corticosteroid sprays are often the first-line recommendation because they target allergy symptoms at the source. It’s best to start using them before pollen season is in full swing (ideally a week or two in advance) and use them consistently for maximum benefit.

Decongestants can help shrink swollen nasal tissues. Options include oral pseudoephedrine or phenylephrine, and nasal sprays like oxymetazoline. They can provide quick relief of a blocked nose.

Eye drops can soothe itchy, watery eyes. Antihistamine eye drops (ketotifen, olopatadine) are available over the counter and provide quick relief. Redness-reducing drops may also soothe irritation.

If you wear contact lenses and have eye allergy symptoms, ask a pharmacist or doctor about the best eye drop option and consider wearing glasses during high-pollen days.

Leukotriene modifiers block leukotrienes, another chemical involved in allergic reactions. Montelukast is a prescription tablet that blocks leukotrienes. It’s sometimes prescribed when antihistamines or nasal sprays alone aren’t enough, or if you have co-existing mild asthma triggered by pollen

Immunotherapy (allergy shots or tablets) offers long-term relief for severe or hard-to-manage seasonal allergies. Under an allergist’s supervision, you’re regularly given small doses of the allergens (like pollen) to gradually reduce your sensitivity.

While immunotherapy doesn’t provide immediate relief, over time – typically 3–5 years – it significantly decreases your allergic reactions, making allergy season much easier to handle.

Understanding and proactively managing seasonal allergies empowers you to maintain your health, comfort, and daily routines.

And if you…

…you can transform allergy season from a period of discomfort into a much more manageable experience.

With our experience of living in Texas as seasoned doctors dealing with allergy season every year, we’re well placed to discuss with you which kind of medication will best relieve your symptoms. You don’t have to suffer every year! Our primary care doctors can help!

We have same-day appointments to suit your lifestyle – citas el mismo día – and primary care is available at all our locations. Come in and let’s find a solution together!

heading: What is an Annual Exam for Women?
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Don’t put off booking your annual exam for women – early detection and preventive care are key to your long-term health. Learn why.
tags: annual exam for women; annual physical
full_text:
One of the most vital steps for long-term wellness is attending your annual exam for women, often part of a broader wellness checkup. In this article we’ll have a look at what’s involved so you feel comfortable attending.

An annual women’s health exam is simply a proactive health check that allows you to address both physical and emotional well-being. It covers various services, including reproductive health, general wellness, and preventive care.

The aim of regular annual exams is to:

Ultimately, these exams are beneficial for you. They help detect conditions early, which allows for more effective and less invasive treatment options.

We’ll look at each part in turn so you’ll know exactly what to expect.

Your annual exam for women will usually begin with discussing your medical history, including any changes or new symptoms since your last visit. Your healthcare provider may ask about:

A physical exam may include checking your vital signs like blood pressure, heart rate, and weight. Your healthcare provider will also assess your general physical health, including skin health, heart, and lungs. They may recommend further tests or screenings if you’re experiencing any specific health issues.

Breast cancer is one of the most common cancers among women, making regular breast exams an essential part of your annual exam. It’s nothing to be scared of and hopefully you, too, have been checking for changes in your breast once a month.

During a clinical breast exam, your healthcare provider will manually examine your breasts and underarms for lumps or abnormalities. Depending on your age and risk factors, they may recommend additional imaging tests, such as a mammogram.

Even when a mammogram shows a lump, it may well be a cyst that can be drained. So, please don’t miss your wellness check for fear of breast cancer.

A pelvic exam is another fundamental component of the annual exam for women. This allows your doctor to check your reproductive organs, including uterus, ovaries, and cervix, for any abnormalities to help identify conditions like ovarian cysts, fibroids, or early signs of reproductive cancers.

A pap smear test is commonly conducted during a pelvic exam and is crucial for detecting early signs of cervical cancer. If you’re searching for a “pap smear test near me,” many healthcare providers and specialized clinics offer this service as part of your annual exam, so it makes sense to attend it!

Here are some other things you might discuss.

Sexual and Reproductive Health

The annual exam is an opportunity to discuss your sexual health concerns, contraception, and family planning. Whether you’re planning to start a family, are concerned about your fertility, or want to discuss birth control options, your healthcare provider can guide you through your choices and recommend the best options based on your health and personal goals.

STI Screening

If you’re sexually active, your doctor may recommend screening for sexually transmitted infections (STIs) as part of your annual women’s health exam. You can also request STI testing if you have any specific concerns or have had new sexual partners.

Menopause Management

If you’re approaching or going through menopause, annual exams are crucial for managing the symptoms and associated health risks. Your doctor can offer support – such BioTE hormone replacement therapy – to manage common symptoms such as hot flashes, mood swings, and changes in sexual function.

Mental Health Support

Many women face challenges such as anxiety, depression, or stress at different life stages, and your healthcare provider can offer you advice, counseling referrals, or treatment options to support your mental well-being.

An annual physical and a well-woman exam are two distinct types of health evaluations. An annual physical focuses on overall health and preventive care for general conditions (including blood tests, cholesterol checks, etc.). A well-woman exam specifically centers on reproductive and sexual health.

Many women opt to have both exams, as they complement each other and ensure a comprehensive health assessment.

Locating a good healthcare provider for your annual exam is one of the most important steps to ensure you get the care you need. If you’re searching for a “women’s annual exam near me,” consider your primary care physician, gynecologist, or health clinic. We’re happy to welcome you to a Stella Mattina clinic for compassionate empathetic care.

Scheduling and attending your annual exam can sometimes come with challenges! Here are some common difficulties you may face and tips to overcome them:

Time Constraints and Busy Schedules

Look for a clinic that offers early morning or evening appointments to fit your schedule – or same-day appointments if you suddenly find a free moment.

Insurance Coverage and Costs

Check with your provider to confirm what’s covered. Most insurance plans are required to cover preventive services such as pap smears and mammograms.

Anxiety or Fear of Discomfort

Look for a provider who specializes in women’s health. They are sensitive to these feelings and can explain each step of the exam beforehand to help you feel more at ease.

Difficulty Finding the Right Provider

If you’re struggling to find the right healthcare provider for your needs, a quick search for “Dallas ObGyn” or another relevant location-based query can provide a list of women’s specialists in your area.

Don’t put off booking your annual exam – early detection and preventive care are key to your long-term health. At Stella Mattina, we specialize in Women’s Health and preventive primary care – plus we offer same-day appointments and accept a comprehensive list of insurance providers, including Medicare and Medicaid.

We make it as easy as possible for you to stay healthy!

Schedule your appointment today.

heading: What is Hepatitis B?
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Hepatitis B is a preventable and potentially serious infection. If you think you've been exposed, take a hep B test and get monitored.
tags: can hep B be prevented?; How does hep B affect your liver; Test for hepatitis; What is hepatitis B?
full_text:
Hepatitis B (or hep B) is a serious liver infection caused by the hepatitis B virus (HBV). This infection can range from a short-term illness to a long-lasting, chronic disease.

Because hep B increases the risk of liver failure, liver cancer, or cirrhosis, it’s helpful to understand how it spreads, and how it is diagnosed and treated. You can then protect your liver health and prevent complications.

Hepatitis means inflammation of the liver. Viral hepatitis is caused by several distinct viruses – the most common being hepatitis A, B, and C. While all three target your liver, they do so in different ways:

All these forms of viral hepatitis cause inflammation. And inflammation harms your liver’s ability to process nutrients, filter toxins, and fight infections.

However, hep B stands out for its potential to become chronic and silently damage your liver over decades if you leave it untreated.

The virus is transmitted, as we mentioned, through contact with infected blood or body fluids.

This can happen through unprotected sex, sharing needles, accidental needle sticks, or from an infected mother to her baby during birth.

Hep B can also spread when you share personal items, such as razors or toothbrushes, with someone who has the infection – although this is less common.

Yes. It’s considered a sexually transmitted disease (STD) since it can be passed through unprotected sexual contact.

If you suspect exposure, it’s crucial you recognize hepatitis B STD symptoms, although many people don’t develop noticeable symptoms at all. When symptoms do appear, they may include fatigue, nausea, jaundice (yellowing of the skin or eyes), dark urine, and joint pain.

Testing starts with a simple blood test and typically includes:

If you want to check if a previous vaccine is protecting you, a hepatitis B immunity test can confirm whether you still have protective antibodies.

Testing is quick and reliable, so take advantage of a test for peace of mind!

Hepatitis B is sometimes called a “silent” infection because many people experience no symptoms for years.

Without treatment, the virus can quietly cause long-term liver inflammation and even progress to cirrhosis or liver cancer. So, even if you feel healthy, if you’ve had any potential exposure, it’s a good idea to discuss your risk factors with your doctor and ask about a hepatitis B test.

Early diagnosis gives you options to protect your health and also prevent the virus from spreading to others.

If you have risk factors like unprotected sex, a partner with hepatitis, shared needles, or you were born in an area with higher hep B rates, you should strongly consider testing.

Newborns of mothers with hepatitis B should be tested and vaccinated immediately after birth.

It’s also wise for healthcare workers and people who live with someone who has hep B to access screening. If you’re unsure, ask your doctor about testing for hepatitis during your next checkup.

If you test positive for hep B, your doctor will guide you through the next steps.

Not everyone needs treatment right away, but you will need regular monitoring to watch for liver damage. Antiviral medications can help control the virus and prevent complications in some cases.

It’s also important to discuss testing and vaccination with partners or close family members so they can get tested and vaccinated if needed. Support groups and counseling can also help you cope emotionally with a hepatitis B diagnosis.

Yes. The good news is you can prevent hep B via vaccination. The vaccine is safe, effective, and provides lifelong protection for most people.

Infants typically receive their first vaccinations at birth. And catch-up vaccines are available for teens and adults who missed their childhood doses.

You can also help prevent hepatitis B transmission by practicing safer sex, avoiding sharing needles, and using standard safety precautions if you work around blood.

However, not everyone knows that hep B is preventable, so…

Globally, World Hepatitis Day was earlier this week. In the US, we also marked Hepatitis Awareness Month in May.

The thing is, awareness plays a vital role in fighting viral hepatitis.

It encourages testing, vaccination, and education, and reminds everyone about liver health and the need to eliminate hepatitis as a public health threat.

By spreading knowledge and reducing stigma, these campaigns help more people get tested, get vaccinated, and stay healthy.

At Stella Mattina, we’re passionate about your health and about being preventive – helping you take action before you get ill!

Hepatitis B is preventable and it’s a potentially serious infection that affects millions of people worldwide.

If you have reason to think you might have been exposed, don’t wait. Call us about having a hep B test or even a full screening that includes a hepatitis C test. We have same-day appointments and accept most insurance.

We have at least 10 locations: Schedule an appointment at the most convenient for you today!

heading: What is the Difference Between Acute and Chronic Conditions?
author: Asma Bhaidani, PA-C
meta_description: Learn how acute and chronic conditions affect your health in different ways so you understand the kind of care you need – and when.
tags: what are acute conditions; what are chronic conditions
full_text:
Acute and chronic conditions affect your health in very different ways and have different timelines. Understanding how they work helps you know when to book an appointment and what kind of care you might need.

So, if you’ve ever wondered what is the difference between acute and chronic conditions, you’re in the right place.

Acute issues come on quickly, change quickly, and usually resolve within days or weeks.

Chronic conditions last longer, usually defined as longer than a year, and often for life. They need ongoing management so they don’t interfere with your daily routine.

You may also hear doctors talk about “acute chronic conditions.” This is when something short-term flares up because of something long-term in the background. For example, a sudden asthma attack when you live with asthma, or high blood pressure spiking during a stressful week.

Knowing the difference between acute and chronic conditions helps you understand what’s happening in your body and when to reach out for care.

Acute conditions often make themselves known right away. Examples include catching a common cold, or coming down with flu, a strep throat, or falling and breaking your wrist. These changes tend to be noticeable, disruptive, and new.

They usually have a clear cause: an infection, an injury, or exposure to someone else who’s sick.

Most acute conditions settle with rest, hydration, or simple medicines. One example is having sneezes and congestion during the peak allergy season.

But some deserve quicker medical care – like a high fever that won’t ease, a cough that gets worse instead of better, or an injury that makes it hard to move.

Primary care clinics handle acute appointments every day. If something feels off or painful and you want clarity, they can usually see you quickly, give you a diagnosis, and start the right treatment plan.

That’s acute illness. So how is chronic illness different?

Chronic conditions stay with you over the long haul. They might be quiet and manageable for months at a time, then shift when stress rises or life gets busy.

Common long-term issues we see include diabetes, thyroid disorders, high blood pressure, and asthma. Plus PCOS or endometriosis,

Chronic condition care revolves around keeping your health steady. We do this with regular check-ins, the right medication plan, routine lab work, and lifestyle support that fits your life.

One difference between acute and chronic conditions is that small changes can make a big difference for chronic care. It’s good to track your symptoms, know your numbers, notice how sleep, stress, and diet affect you.

The more consistent you are with attending to these details for chronic care, the fewer flare-ups you’ll face.

Preventive care is another big piece of the puzzle.

Many long-term conditions don’t show early symptoms. At Stella Mattina, we’re passionate about preventive care.

That’s why coming in for your annual exams and other appointments is important. If we can spot changes in your blood pressure, blood sugar, or thyroid levels before you feel unwell, you stay healthier for longer and avoid complications.

Your primary care team is the first place to go for both sudden problems and long-term conditions.

Whether you’re searching for a primary care doctor Dallas location or a primary care physician Dallas, you’ll be in good hands.

Why? Because the same clinician who helps you handle a sprained ankle can also help you manage asthma or diabetes. Their training is comprehensive – so they recognize signals that something needs closer attention.

For example, sometimes an acute issue can highlight a chronic one. Someone might come in for headaches and discover high blood pressure. Or a lingering cold might point to an underlying immune issue. When the same team sees you regularly, it’s easier to catch patterns early.

If you need a specialist, your primary care provider can refer you quickly and make sure nothing falls through the cracks.

Getting care at the right moment changes everything.

Acute issues often benefit from early treatment. A sore throat becomes easier to manage when strep is caught sooner. A minor injury heals faster when it’s protected before swelling increases. Quick checks can also rule out anything serious, which gives you peace of mind.

Chronic conditions depend even more on timing.

If you miss appointments for long-term conditions, they can flare up or cause complications.

For example, high blood pressure left unchecked can affect your heart. Diabetes without monitoring can impact your nerves, eyes, and kidneys. Thyroid problems that go untreated can slow your metabolism, mood, and energy.

We don’t expect perfection! Life gets busy. Things slip. What matters is that you come in when something feels wrong, or when we recommend your next review.

There’s no such thing as a “silly” appointment. If something worries you, causes discomfort, or simply feels unusual for your body, it’s worth getting checked.

Come in soon for things like:

For long-term conditions – like diabetes, high blood pressure, thyroid disorders, or asthma – routine follow-ups keep things steady.

These visits help you understand what’s happening in your body, why you may need to adjust your medication, and how to stay ahead of any complications.

Now you understand the difference between acute and chronic conditions, you have more control over your health.

At Stella Mattina, we support you when you need it – whether that’s for something new that popped up or a change in a long-term condition.

Same-day visits are available if you have a sudden concern. And our ongoing chronic care plans help you stay on track and avoid unnecessary complications.

Booking an appointment at any of our Dallas locations is easy. You can book online whenever you’re ready.

heading: What to Expect from a Women’s Healthcare Specialist
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Learn how a women's healthcare specialist such as a gynecologist or obstetrician can meet your needs with a comprehensive, tailored service.
tags: gynecologist; obstetrician; women's healthcare specialist
full_text:
You may be wondering if choosing a women’s healthcare specialist rather than a general doctor is a good move for you. And if you’re like many women, you may not be entirely clear about all the different medical roles there are in women’s healthcare that you may come across!

For example, here’s a list of some of those specialists:

Your primary care doctor may, for example, refer you to a gynecologist and obstetrician. But all these specialists work together, consulting with each other when necessary to ensure you receive the best possible care as a woman.

In this post, however, we’ll talk in detail about the role of gynecologists and obstetricians and the roles they play when you attend advanced women’s health centers. There’s some overlap and we’ll point that out too.

Routine Examinations and Preventive Care:

This includes things you should have regularly, such as pelvic exams, Pap smears, breast exams, and screenings for sexually transmitted infections. Your gynecologist, as an women’s healthcare specialist, is experienced in – and alert for – catching any issues early to give you the best chance of resolving the problem.

If you need advice on family planning and contraceptive options so that you can have children at a time of your choosing – or not at all – a gynecologist is your go-to healthcare specialist. But an obstetrician will also help with these issues. It makes sense to address future family planning at the same time if they’re already involved with you during pregnancy and postpartum care!

Your gynecologist will be involved in addressing issues you may have. These night include menstrual disorders, pelvic pain, fibroids, endometriosis, ovarian cysts, or infections. If necessary, they can consult with the fertility specialists and pelvic pain specialists we mentioned up top to ensure the best outcomes for you.

Perhaps the role people mostly associate with gynecologists is that of performing surgeries such as hysterectomies, laparoscopies, and other procedures related to reproductive health. But as women’s healthcare specialists, they have a far wider skill set! For example…

One of the roles you might not think of as belonging to gynecology is assisting you with those annoying symptoms of menopause. The ones that can make you feel tired and irritable as you enter the perimenopause years! You might want to talk about, for example, vaginal dryness affecting your sex life. As a women’s healthcare specialist, your gynecologist can discuss how hormone therapy may help. They can also offer information about other health changes that occur during these years and how to manage them – or refer you to a menopause specialist.

Your gynecologist is expert in any aspect of your health as a woman that has an effect on your reproductive or sexual health. This might include diet, exercise, or lifestyle choices.

Remember that, before they specialized, all gynecologists and obstetricians underwent lengthy medical training and are conversant with many issues! But let’s turn to obstetricians now.

Pregnancy Care:

When thinking about ObGyn care, you probably know that an obstetrician will often undertake aspects of your prenatal care and testing. This includes monitoring the health of you and the fetus growing inside you, and managing any pregnancy related condition. They can also provide birth plans. However, you may also have a midwife who will be with you during your labor and birth. They, too, can help you plan in advance for those events.

An obstetrician will manage your labor and delivery, and especially be involved in cesarean deliveries.

As part of your women’s healthcare ObGyn team, your obstetrician will follow up with care after childbirth, including physical and emotional support – especially if you’ve had a difficult or high-risk pregnancy. An example might be if you need help with postpartum hemorrhage management.

Your obstetric specialist can give you advice on family planning and fertility evaluations. But see above and below, as gynecologists also do this!

As a women’s healthcare specialist, your obstetrician can give advice about pregnancy-related and childbirth issues, coordinating among their colleagues to get you the best care for you.

Many specialists in this field consult with related specialists to bring you the best health outcomes. And many are trained in both disciplines (ObGyn) and also alongside midwives, so that their role in women’s healthcare can be professionally interdisciplinary.

For example, as we’ve seen, both a gynecologist and an obstetrician can be involved in

So – when might you see one or the other? This depends on what you’re expecting them to help you with, and perhaps whether you’re already under the care of one of them.

Gynecologists are well versed in various contraceptive options and can help in selecting the one that best suits your health and lifestyle. They can help with fertility concerns you may have, such as discussing menstrual cycle irregularities, sexual health, or pre-conception health. They can also conduct initial assessments and provide guidance on how to optimize your fertility.

Obstetricians often handle cases that involve pregnancy management, including fertility treatments like IVF (In Vitro Fertilization). When planning for a pregnancy – especially if you have a known medical condition or previous pregnancy complication – an obstetrician can provide you with more targeted advice and care plans.

If you want the best women’s healthcare possible in both primary care and ObGyn in Dallas, Stella Mattina prides itself on easy-to-access, comprehensive medical services for all women. We offer appointments as early as today, accept most insurances, and tailor our services to your unique needs as a woman.

Looking for a women’s healthcare specialist to be sure your ObGyn needs are met with compassion by board-certified physicians?

Contact us today and let’s discuss your unique requirements.

heading: When is the Best Time to Test for Hormone Imbalance?
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Your health depends on hormones working effecively. Empower yourself by learning when and why to test for hormone imbalance.
tags: HRT for hormone imbalance; types of test for hormone imbalance; when to test for for hormone imbalance
full_text:
Understanding your body’s hormonal balance is essential for optimal health and well-being. Hormones regulate many of your body’s processes, and imbalances can significantly impact your quality of life. This article will help you identify when to test for hormone imbalance, recognize vital symptoms, and manage any imbalances effectively, empowering you to take control of your health.

Hormonal imbalances can affect individuals of any age or gender. They can be triggered by various factors, including stress, diet, medical conditions, and life stages such as pregnancy and menopause.

Testing for hormone imbalance is essential if you suspect a problem because hormones are crucial in every bodily function. We want you to enjoy optimal health!

Imbalances can result in a wide range of symptoms and health issues, including fatigue, weight gain, mood swings, and reproductive problems.

When you have a test for hormone imbalance, you and your doctor can identify the underlying causes of your symptoms and take appropriate steps to correct them. This ultimately improves the symptoms and you feel well again.

Hormonal imbalances can show up in various ways, depending on which hormones are affected. Common symptoms include:

Certain life stages and conditions can predispose you to hormone imbalances. Some of these stages and conditions include:

We’ll look at four specific occasions when it might be good to check for hormone imbalance. But please remember that any time you suspect a hormone imbalance could be at the root of your problems, do please come in and talk to us.

Postpartum hormonal changes can lead to imbalances that may affect both physical and mental health. Signs of hormone imbalance after pregnancy include

Testing for hormone imbalance during the postpartum period can help address these issues and support recovery.

Hormonal testing can be particularly important for you if you’re experiencing irregular menstrual cycles, heavy bleeding, or fertility issues.

The best time to check for hormonal imbalances depends on the problems you’re having, and if fertility is a concern. A detailed history from your provider will help guide us to specific tests needed to resolve your concerns.

These years are significant periods of hormonal change. A full hormone panel, which includes estrogen, progesterone, and testosterone tests, is often recommended to get a complete picture of your hormonal health.

Testing for hormone imbalance during perimenopause can help manage symptoms like hot flashes, mood swings, and sleep disturbances.

In addition, recent research confirms certain benefits of hormone replacement therapy after 65 years, according to type, route, and strength, so it’s worth considering.

Even if you’re not experiencing specific symptoms, regular hormonal testing can be beneficial for maintaining overall health. This is especially true if you have a family history of hormonal disorders or other risk factors. Regular testing can help catch imbalances early and prevent potential health problems.

Blood Tests

Blood tests are the most common method for testing hormone levels. They accurately measure various hormones, including thyroid hormones, estrogen, progesterone, testosterone, and cortisol.

Urine Tests

Urine tests can measure hormone metabolites, providing a comprehensive view of hormone production and metabolism. Doctors often use these tests to assess adrenal function and identify hormone imbalances like cortisol and aldosterone.

Finding a reliable place for hormonal testing is essential for accurate results. Searching for “hormonal testing near me” can help you locate reputable clinics and laboratories in your area.

Many healthcare providers, including endocrinologists, gynecologists, and general practitioners, offer hormone testing. Some specialized clinics also provide comprehensive hormonal testing and personalized treatment plans.

Lifestyle changes are often the first line of defense against hormonal imbalances. These can include:

HRT is a standard treatment for hormonal imbalances, especially during menopause. It involves supplementing your body with hormones to balance levels and alleviate symptoms.

How long after starting HRT do you feel a difference?

This is a common question and the answer varies from person to person. But many begin to notice improvements within a few weeks to a few months of starting treatment.

One option for HRT is hormone pellets. At Stella Mattina we use BioTe pellets. These small, rice-sized pellets are implanted under the skin and gradually release hormones. Female hormone pellets can provide a steady supply of hormones and are often used to treat symptoms of menopause.

It’s important, also, to be aware of the possible side effects of hormone pellets in females, including infection at the implantation site, changes in mood, and weight gain.

Monitoring and Adjusting Treatment

Once you begin treatment for hormonal imbalance, regular testing is crucial to monitor your hormone levels and adjust your treatment as needed. This ensures that your hormone levels remain balanced and that you get the maximum benefit from your treatment.

Make sure to work closely with your healthcare provider, which is essential for managing hormonal imbalances. They can help you understand your test results, adjust treatment plans, and address any side effects or concerns.

Do remember to communicate openly with your provider about your symptoms and how you’re feeling. An expert women’s health provider fully understands the nuances of hormone treatments.

Take control of your health by scheduling a test for hormone imbalance today. At Stella Mattina, we’re passionate about every aspect of women’s health. We aim to provide care when you need it, and not just when we’re available. We have a large provider network that can offer you an appointment as soon as today.

Contact us now and schedule that appointment!

heading: When Should You Get Your First Mammogram?
author: Dr. Theodore J. T. Krum
meta_description: Learn when to schedule your first mammogram so you benefit from detecting early breast cancer symptoms, which are then treatable.
tags: breast cancer awareness; first mammogram; how to self-examine for breast cancer; importance of mammograms; mammogram vs ultrasound
full_text:
We’re writing this post in October, which is Breast Cancer Awareness Month. Early detection of breast cancer can significantly increase your chances of successful treatment. You probably know that one of the most effective methods for early detection is a mammogram. However, you may be unsure about when to schedule your first mammogram. So let’s look at:

A mammogram is an X-ray image of your breast to screen for breast cancer. It can usually detect early signs of breast cancer before you can see or feel a symptom.

As mentioned up top, early detection is key in successfully treating breast cancer – which is why scheduling mammogram screening at the right age is important for your health.

How do you know when to get your first mammogram? Recommendations can vary depending on various factors, including your age, family history, and overall health.

Women aged 40 to 44

It’s generally considered optional to start screening with a mammogram at this age. You may choose to begin annual mammograms if you want to be proactive, but it’s important to discuss your risk factors with a healthcare provider before making this decision.

A good reason to have that personal discussion is that some doctors and screeners disagree with the US Preventive Services Task Force’s recently updated recommendation that all women with average risk should start screening at age 40. They argue that there are many more false positives at that age, which induce anxiety. So – talk to your doctor!

Women aged 45 to 54

Annual mammograms are typically advisable for this age group, as the risk of breast cancer increases significantly during these years.

Women aged 55 and older

At this age, you can switch to mammograms every two years, though you may continue with annual screenings if you prefer.

If you have a higher risk of breast cancer – for example, if you have a family history of the disease or a genetic predisposition – doctors may recommend starting mammograms earlier, sometimes as early as age 30.

At Stella Mattina, we advise consulting with your healthcare provider about your risk factors, as that’s crucial in determining the best screening schedule for you.

Figuring out where to get your first mammogram? Mammograms are widely available at hospitals, specialized breast clinics, and imaging centers. Here’s how you can find a facility near you:

October is widely recognized as Breast Cancer Awareness Month, a time when organizations worldwide aim to raise awareness about breast cancer, its risks, and the importance of early detection.

During October Breast Cancer Awareness campaigns, you may see a heightened focus on screening programs, fundraising for research, and educational content designed to empower you to take charge of your breast health.

This is therefore an ideal time to schedule your first mammogram, as many clinics and healthcare organizations offer discounted or free mammograms to promote early detection. Participating in these initiatives can help you stay proactive about health.

In addition to mammograms, you may undergo breast ultrasounds as part of your normal screening process.

Ultrasounds use sound waves to produce images of breast tissue and to help with a further investigation into a suspected abnormality found on a mammogram or physical exam.

So, how does breast cancer on ultrasound differ from what a mammogram shows?

Mammograms are effective at detecting tiny calcium deposits (microcalcifications), which can be an early sign of cancer. They also capture dense breast tissue and reveal tumors that are too small to be felt.

Ultrasounds are more effective at distinguishing between solid tumors and fluid-filled cysts. They’re often used as a supplementary tool when mammogram results are unclear or if the breast tissue is particularly dense, making it difficult to see abnormalities.

Along with knowing when to schedule your first mammogram, one of the most important habits to develop is regularly checking your breasts for any unusual changes.

Here’s a step-by-step guide:

Look out for any lumps, thickening, or changes in texture. While most breast changes are not cancerous, it’s important to report any concerns to your healthcare provider as soon as possible.

Whether you’re scheduling your first mammogram or returning for a follow-up, we’ll help you stay informed and proactive about your breast health. It’s one of the most empowering things you can do for yourself. Schedule a visit today!

heading: When to Consider Fertility Testing in Your 20s and 30s
author: Dr. Gonzalo Venegas
meta_description: Fertility testing gives you a clearer picture of where you stand today – and what to expect tomorrow. Learn everything you need to know.
tags: how fertility changes after 30; how to test fertility; when to see a fertility specialist; why fertility testing matters
full_text:
If you haven’t yet decided to have children but plan to do so, you may find yourself thinking about fertility testing in your 20s and 30s. It’s always good to understand your options for the future.

And even if starting a family is NOT on your mind right now, learning more about your fertility can help you make informed choices later.

The thing is, your reproductive health doesn’t stay the same forever. So, testing can give you a clearer picture of where you stand today – and what to expect tomorrow.

In this article we’ll look at

Your 20s are usually when fertility is at its strongest. But not everyone’s body follows the same pattern.

Conditions like PCOS and endometriosis, irregular cycles, or even your family history can affect fertility without obvious signs.

It’s also possible that an STD (such as chlamydia) – even without symptoms – is causing inflammation that affects your later fertility.

Smoking, drinking, and drug use, etc., can affect your fertility – and obesity, too, can impact your chances of conception.

However, taking a fertility test for women in their 20s doesn’t imply you’re assuming you have a problem. You’re simply gathering the information you need to plan ahead confidently. This is especially important if you’re thinking about delaying pregnancy for various reasons.

Simple ovarian reserve testing (which looks at how many eggs you’re likely to have left) can be a useful baseline for the future.

And knowing your hormone levels, like AMH, and seeing how your ovaries are functioning can also reassure you that everything is on track. Or perhaps alert you to things you and your doctor can address early and treat successfully.

The truth is, though, that fertility starts to decline as you enter your 30s, more noticeably after 35! That’s why people refer to the “biological clock” ticking down. And it’s worth understanding what that timeline can mean for you.

A fertility assessment in your early 30s can help you see whether your body is following the typical pattern – or if you might need to make different plans.

You might also want to use this information to decide if egg freezing is a good option for you.

If you’re already closer to your late 30s and unsure about your fertility, your provider may also start discussing appropriate fertility treatments for women over 35.

But fertility testing is the first step to deciding what, if anything, you need to do.

Fertility testing doesn’t have to be complicated. For most women, it starts with a few simple steps.

Your healthcare provider may recommend bloodwork to check for hormones and markers related to fertility (see here for more detail). But in brief these include:

Other tests include a transvaginal ultrasound to check how your ovaries and uterus look and help estimate the number of eggs you might still have.

A hysterosalpingogram (HSG) can X-ray your reproductive organs.

Finally, your doctor can examine your uterus through your cervix – hysteroscopy.

Every woman is different, so fertility testing really depends on your symptoms, medical history, and goals.

If you have specific concerns – like irregular periods, a history of endometriosis, or a family history of early menopause – your doctor can help decide what tests make sense for you.

And don’t forget to talk to your partner about fertility tests. Testing male fertility starts with sperm count, sperm motility, and sperm size and shape. It’s good for both of you to know your status!

For most women, an ObGyn or primary care doctor is a good place to start. But there are times when seeing a fertility specialist is the next logical step.

If you’ve been trying to get pregnant for a year (or six months if you’re over 35) without success, or if you have known conditions like PCOS, it’s worth asking for a referral.

Even if you’re not actively trying to start a family, your provider may recommend a specialist if your fertility testing results show a lower ovarian reserve than expected. This keeps you informed and your options open.

You can also ask about fertility testing near me to find local options and decide where you feel most comfortable getting tested.

You don’t have to wait until you’re ready to have a baby to bring up the topic of fertility! It’s completely okay to tell your doctor you just want to understand your options and be prepared for the future.

So, at your next visit, you could ask about the tests they recommend for you right now and how often you might need to recheck your levels.

If your results show anything unusual, you can also talk through whether seeing a fertility specialist now or later makes most sense for you.

Fertility testing is simply about giving you more choices.

Whatever your biological timeline looks like, you deserve to know what’s going on with your body so you can plan your next steps with confidence.

If you’re ready to learn more about your fertility in your 20s or 30s, our ObGyns at Stella Mattina are specialists in women’s health.

We have many convenient locations, experienced providers, and a policy of same-day appointments to suit your lifestyle. We also speak Spanish.

Get the answers you need from our easy-to-access healthcare and receive expert support to plan your next steps.

Whether you’re just curious or already thinking about growing your family, our team is here to help you feel confident about your reproductive health whatever your age. Choose your location and let’s talk!

He has led the Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology at Methodist Dallas Medical Center and contributed to community health through his role on the Parkland Health & Hospital System Board. A dedicated mentor at UT Southwestern, Dr. Venegas is known for his leadership, compassion, and commitment to advancing women’s health and medical education.

heading: Women’s Health and Menopause Guide – for Every Stage of the Transition
author: Dr. Gonzalo Venegas
meta_description: Learn about women's health and the five pillars of menopause wellness to help you thrive through every stage of menopause transitions.
tags: health and menopause guide; stages of menopause
full_text:
Menopause is a natural shift. Yet it can affect your physical, emotional, and long-term health.

The menopause transition isn’t one moment.

It unfolds gradually as part of your “midlife women’s health experience!” Menstrual periods, sleep, and moods can all go awry.

Many women notice hot flashes, vaginal dryness, or new patterns in their weight or energy.

Because this menopause transition influences so many areas at once, it’s an important time to think more intentionally about women’s health. Your heart, bones, brain, and metabolism all respond to shifting hormones. And when your life’s busy, it’s easy to ignore symptoms.

This guide explains how menopause works, how to recognize it, what the stages look like, which menopause symptoms tend to be most challenging, and how to care for your body through every stage.

Read on to learn about women’s health, menopause transitions, and how to manage and treat menopause effectively.

Table of contents What is Menopause and Why Does It Matter for Your Health? How to Know You’re Going Through Menopause The Stages of Menopause Common and Challenging Menopausal Symptoms The Five Pillars of Menopause Wellness How to Take Care of Your Body During Menopausal Transitions Which Doctors Help With Menopause?

Menopause is a natural biological process that marks the end of your reproductive years. It happens when your ovaries slow their hormone production, especially estrogen and progesterone, and your menstrual periods stop for good.

These hormonal shifts aren’t sudden. Your hormone levels fluctuate for years before your final period. Which is why so many physical and emotional changes appear gradually.

Most women reach natural menopause between ages 45 and 55, although genetics, health conditions, surgeries, and lifestyle factors can all influence timing.

But some women experience early menopause, while others notice changes later. Wherever you fall, this transition affects far more than your reproductive years!

Menopause is a meaningful phase in women’s health because changing hormones influence long-term wellbeing. Bone strength, cardiovascular health, metabolic balance, sexual comfort, and mental wellness all respond to these shifts.

If you don’t manage your symptoms, they can contribute to issues like osteoporosis or increased heart disease risk over time.

Understanding how the menopause transition works – and knowing what’s normal for each stage – helps you manage symptoms, stay active, and feel more in control of the changes ahead.

Most women don’t wake up one morning and think, “It’s starting.” You may only recognize the menopause transition when a few changes stack up over time.

Shifts in your menstrual periods are often the earliest clue. Cycles may become shorter, longer, lighter, or heavier – i.e. unpredictable.

But you might notice earlier symptoms like sleep disruption, mood swings, hot flashes, or new sensitivity to stress. Some women experience subtle cognitive shifts, like trouble concentrating. Or they begin to experience menopausal symptoms that don’t seem connected at first.

Menopause becomes “official” when you’ve gone 12 straight months without a period. This marks the moment your ovaries have slowed their hormone output enough that hormonal changes stabilize into a new pattern.

But the years leading up to that point – called perimenopause – tend to be the most noticeable in terms of symptoms.

As for timing, most women enter this phase in their mid-40s to early 50s, but there’s a wide normal range.

Genetics, surgeries, health conditions, and lifestyle factors all play a part. If you’re wondering “How early can menopause start?”, the answer is that some women notice changes in their late 30s.

Any shift that feels unusual or disruptive is worth discussing with a healthcare professional.

The menopause transition unfolds over years, so understanding the stages helps you make sense of it.

Each phase brings different hormonal shifts, timing of menstrual periods, and menopause symptoms. There’s no universal timeline, but the stages follow the same overall sequence for most women.

Pre-menopause is the stage when your hormones still follow their regular rhythm. No noticeable menopausal symptoms. Your periods arrive on time.

Perimenopause is where most women begin to feel change. Hormone levels rise and fall unevenly, so symptoms can be unpredictable. Hot flashes, sleep disruption, mood changes, and shifts in bleeding patterns show up. This stage can last several years, and symptoms tend to ebb and flow.

Menopause is a single point in time: the day you reach 12 months without a period. You may still have symptoms, but hormonally, it marks your body settling into a more consistent pattern. It’s also the answer to a question many people ask: “What signals the end of menopause?” It’s this moment: no more periods.

Early postmenopause includes the first few years after menopause. Some symptoms may improve, but certain changes continue. Bone density, cardiovascular strength, and metabolic health are especially important to monitor during this stage. Why? Because hormonal changes can influence long-term outcomes like osteoporosis and heart disease.

Late postmenopause focuses on healthy longevity. Hormones have already settled. Your priority is staying active, protecting bone and heart health, and addressing ongoing symptoms if they persist. With good support and proactive care, these years can feel stable and strong.

You move through the menopause transition at your own pace. The stages don’t follow a strict schedule, yet research and clinical experience give us a general guide.

Pre-menopause lasts from your teens through your 30s and early 40s. Hormones stay fairly steady, your menstrual periods follow your usual rhythm, and menopause symptoms haven’t started yet.

Perimenopause is the most variable stage. It can last two or three years or closer to a decade. Fluctuating hormone levels cause symptoms to come and go. This is also the stage where questions like “How long does menopause last?” tend to come up, because the shifting cycles and menopausal symptoms can feel frustrating.

Menopause itself is only a moment in time, as we said. But the symptoms surrounding it may continue for several years.

Early postmenopause generally lasts the first four to five years after menopause. Hot flashes, sleep changes, and other symptoms may ease or continue depending on how your body settles into its new hormonal pattern.

Late postmenopause includes everything after those early years. Long-term health planning matters for bone strength, heart protection, and overall metabolic health.

If anything feels unusual, do talk to a menopause healthcare professional or healthcare providers familiar with menopause.

The menopause transition varies but many patterns show up again and again.

Hormone levels rise and fall unevenly during menopause and can cause mild shifts. But you may experience menopausal symptoms that disrupt daily life.

Hot flashes are the symptom most women talk about – often paired with night sweats that interrupt sleep, leaving you drained.

Vaginal dryness is also common. When estrogen declines, your vaginal tissue becomes less elastic or lubricated. Intercourse can be uncomfortable with the risk of irritation.

Changes to your menstrual periods can be equally challenging. Maybe with heavy bleeding – or months without a period, then an unexpected one. This is perimenopause! Often paired with cramps, headaches, or mood shifts.

These mood swings, anxiety, irritability, or feeling less emotionally steady aren’t “in your head.” They’re tied to hormonal changes affecting neurotransmitters that regulate stress and emotional balance.

Sexual desire may change during the menopause transition, and the combination of low estrogen, dryness, and reduced sleep can make intimacy unwelcome.

Patients tell us these common menopause symptoms (hot flashes, disrupted sleep, vaginal discomfort, and unpredictable cycles) reduce their quality of life.

But healthcare providers who understand menopause – including a menopause specialist or a clinician familiar with midlife care – can help you manage symptoms. Treatment options range from lifestyle changes to evidence-based approaches like hormone therapy, depending on your goals and health background.

Even though the menopause transition can feel unpredictable, there are five core pillars or habits that improve daily life and long-term health. Whether your menopause symptoms are mild, moderate, or frustratingly persistent, give them a try!

Nutrition

The right nutrition helps stabilize your energy and reduce menopausal symptoms like bloating, hot flashes, or shifts in weight or mood during menopause. A balanced approach with protein, fiber, healthy fats, and calcium-rich foods supports your bones, muscles, and overall wellbeing.

Movement

Regular movement protects bone strength, supports a healthy heart, and improves sleep. Strength training becomes especially important in midlife because it helps counteract natural muscle loss and reduces osteoporosis risk. Even short bursts of activity can help you manage symptoms.

Rest and Recovery

Sleep can become more difficult during perimenopause and post menopause. Build a wind-down routine, adjust your sleep environment, and keep consistent sleep times to reduce stress and help reduce hot flashes and mood swings.

Mental Health

Shifts in hormones can influence emotional steadiness. Any stress-reduction practices can help make the transition more manageable. Good midlife women’s healthcare includes having someone to talk to during menopause transitions!

Connection Menopause is easier if you connect with friends, family, support groups, or your healthcare providers. Evidence-based group resources from the Menopause Society or local women’s health networks can help you stay informed, feel supported, and know which questions to bring to a menopause specialist.

Together, these pillars help you feel more in control of the transition.

Your body’s needs shift during the menopause transition. With the right support, each stage is more manageable. Adopting small, steady habits often makes the biggest difference to midlife women’s health.

Fitness and Mobility Support

Movement becomes one of your most powerful tools in midlife, as we mentioned. Strength training helps maintain muscle mass, improves balance, and supports your bones so you’re less vulnerable to osteoporosis.

Cardiovascular exercise protects your heart during a life stage where heart disease risk slowly rises. Even light daily activity can improve sleep and help you manage symptoms like hot flashes or mood shifts.

Nutrition Strategies for Hormone Balance

As your hormone levels settle, food choices play an important role in regulating blood sugar, energy, and inflammation.

Many women find it helpful to focus on protein, fiber, calcium, and vitamin D, while limiting foods that trigger menopause symptoms. Hydration and regular meals also help you stay steady during the menopausal transition.

Long-Term Health Planning

The years after menopause are an ideal time to focus on screenings, bone protection, heart health, and emotional wellbeing.

Make sure to work with healthcare providers who understand midlife women’s health. They can help you tailor a plan that fits your lifestyle.

Evidence-based options like hormone therapy may be appropriate for many women. But it depends on your health history and what symptoms you’re facing.

Talk openly with a healthcare professional or menopause specialist to ensure you get care that supports you now and long-term.

If your menopause symptoms interfere with daily life, it’s worth checking in with a healthcare professional who understands the menopause transition.

Many women start with their gynecologist, since they know your history and can guide you through changing menstrual periods, symptom patterns, and overall women’s health needs.

A menopause specialist – including gynecologists with extra training to treat menopause, endocrinologists, and some primary care clinicians – can help you explore options to manage symptoms of menopause such as hot flashes, sleep disruption, or vaginal dryness.

In cities like Dallas, searching for a Menopause specialist Dallas or a clinic focused on Women’s Health Dallas can point you to the right expertise.

Your broader care team may include your primary care doctor, therapists, pelvic floor specialists, or the Menopause Society mentioned above.

Menopause is a natural phase, but support is now more available than ever. When you understand how the transition works – and have women’s health clinicians who take your symptoms seriously – daily life becomes manageable and your long-term health protected.

At Stella Mattina, our healthcare professionals are here to guide you through every stage of your menopause transition. Whether you’re dealing with hot flashes, sleep changes, vaginal dryness, or questions about menopause treatment options, we give you clear, compassionate care that puts your menopause wellbeing first.

We offer same-day appointments. Schedule your visit at one of our locations today.

He has led the Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology at Methodist Dallas Medical Center and contributed to community health through his role on the Parkland Health & Hospital System Board. A dedicated mentor at UT Southwestern, Dr. Venegas is known for his leadership, compassion, and commitment to advancing women’s health and medical education.
